news.title: New NRCC Ad Blasts Socialist Amish Shah As Dangerous for Arizona Families
news.text:
The National Republican Congressional Committee (NRCC) dropped a new paid digital ad today targeting Amish Shah, the radical socialist who just won the primary in Arizona's 1st Congressional District, a key battleground for control of the House. The ad highlights Shah's ties to the Democratic Socialists of America (DSA), including backing Bernie Sanders for president at a DSA event in 2019. He also heaped praise on AOC and other members of the extreme left-wing Squad, praising their progressive movement in the Democratic Party while calling our economic system unfair.

Shah ran for Congress in 2024, where he underperformed both Kamala Harris and Ruben Gallego, and now he's decided that his best move is to run even farther to the left than he did two years ago. Shah himself admitted it back in May, saying, "I’m not hesitating one bit to use, you know, my platform to be able to prosecute as vigorously as I can sometimes with, you know, expletives and whatever it takes" because President Trump is "horrible."

The NRCC's ad highlights Shah's extremism and the danger he poses to Arizona families.

"Socialist Amish Shah," the ad says. "He's with AOC, Ilhan Omar ... The Squad. Amish Shah would raise taxes by $4,000 per year on every family, and give sanctuary to criminal illegal aliens. Shah voted against border security funding, against police funding, and against requiring child sex offenders to register online. Shah even voted for early release for dangerous criminals. Socialist Amish Shah: too extreme, too dangerous for us."

Shah has an uphill battle within his own party, as Democrat leadership grapples with what to do to address the insurgence of socialists. Will the Democrats back Shah financially? The Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee (DCCC) and its allies spent $4.5 million in an effort to defeat Shah, and Shah spent the primary night begging the DCCC for support, going so far as to joke that he hopes to get their endorsement over his Republican opponent, Jay Feely.

That's awkward: Socialist @DrAmishShah spent primary night begging the DCCC for support after the DCCC & its allies spent $4.5 million trying to defeat him.SHAH: "I hope that I can get their endorsement over Jay Feely." #AZ01 https://t.co/LEzIGnHHDZ pic.twitter.com/1z29LOtPRD— Ben Petersen (@bennpetersen) July 22, 2026

Shah admitted earlier this year that the DCCC executive director wanted someone more electable, and even The New York Times said Shah's primary would leave him "bloodied" and "broke" ahead of the general election.

Meanwhile, Feely cruised to victory on election night, earning almost 50 percent of the vote and receiving strong praise from pundits for his disciplined, unifying campaign. Pollster Mike Noble called Feely "one of the best Republican candidates in the state right now." Political consultant Stan Barnes said Feely is a "dynamic & attractive candidate" and that Shah will "end up in second place."

"Socialist Amish Shah would take Arizona down a costly, dangerous path," said NRCC Spokesperson Ben Petersen. "Socialist Shah backed enormous tax hikes on every Arizona household, demanded a socialist government takeover of health care and voted at the State Capitol to put our families in danger from criminals, drug cartels, and child sex offenders."

Editor’s Note: The 2026 Midterms will determine the fate of President Trump’s America First agenda. Republicans must maintain control of both chambers of Congress.

news.author: Amy Curtis

news.title: New Poll Reveals Where AOC Stands In Race For The 2028 Democratic Nomination
news.text:
A new poll released on Wednesday in the all-important primary state of New Hampshire revealed progressive firebrand Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.) leading a crowded pack of potential nominees for the 2028 Democratic presidential ticket. 

Ocasio-Cortez has surged ahead of former Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg among likely 2028 Democratic primary voters in the latest University of New Hampshire poll.

When asked about the results, Ocasio-Cortez expressed her gratitude to voters for putting their confidence in her but emphasized what her main focus currently is. 

“I think there’s two things, one is that you know polls are polls, and a horse race is a horse race. I am, of course, I think very deeply humbled and grateful to you folks who participate and convey that confidence,” Ocasio-Cortez told reporters outside the Capitol on Wednesday.

“I think my main focus is really genuinely just the midterms right now, and we have to make sure that we win this majority,” she continued. 

Ocasio-Cortez has not officially announced her intention to seek the Democratic nomination in 2028, but has consistently appeared in national polling within the top five of potential candidates. 

Other top candidates in national polling include California Gov. Gavin Newsom and former Vice President Kamala Harris. 

New Hampshire is historically considered to be an early indication of a candidate’s success or failure, being that it is the second Democratic primary to take place every election year.
news.author: Anthony Orrico

news.title: Want more black doctors? Improve K-12 education first
news.text:
So you want diversity in California’s top med schools?

Then start by teaching black and brown children — and indeed, all children — to read, write and think.

The US Department of Justice (DOJ) recently accused the University of California, San Diego School of Medicine of race-based discrimination after a four-month investigation. 

The DOJ claims that UCSD favors black and Latino students over white and Asian applicants, using a loophole the school created by asking “hardship questions” that reveal racial identity.

The stakes are high. Last year, the National Institutes of Health awarded UCSD over $400 million for research, which could now be at stake, since federal funding requires compliance with federal regulations. 

Here’s a thought: How about helping all students reach high standards, rather than skirting the law to favor some who don’t quite meet the mark?

The DOJ alleges UCSD’s admissions rate for black applicants (6.18%) was almost three times the rate of whites (2.45%), despite lower median grades and Medical College Admissions Test (MCAT) scores. While administrators defend what they call “merit-based admissions,” critics have said for years that UC schools have been trying to reinsert race into the process, despite the race-neutral requirements of Proposition 209, passed by California voters in 1996.

Medical schools in particular should prioritize merit, given the potential impact of less capable doctors on public health.

The problem is larger than UCSD. We as a society are starting too late by focusing on post-secondary education. The core issue is black and brown student achievement in K-12 schools.

American students are reeling from a public-school education system that places the wants of unionized teachers and administrators above the needs of students.

On Monday, the Annie E. Casey Foundation published its childhood wellness report, “The Kids Count Data Book,” spanning 2019-2024. The results are, frankly, shocking. For example, 70% of America’s fourth-graders are not proficient in reading.

The results are even worse in black and Latino communities. 

By eighth grade, 90% of black children are not proficient in math at grade level. The national average is 73%.

How can anyone hope to score well on the MCAT when he or she never learned to read at grade level?

The report blames the coronavirus pandemic for the deficits in performance, but left-wing ideology and parental apathy are squarely to blame for the lack of attainment.

It is good to see the DOJ and the Trump administration cracking down on discriminatory practices in higher education.

But without major intervention in K-12 schools, there will continue to be fewer American children prepared to score well on the MCAT, LSAT and other professional entrance exams.

There are many good ideas for reform. 

Download The California Post App, follow us on social, and subscribe to our newsletters 

We should consider shifting away from electronic devices and moving back toward old-fashioned, pencil-and-paper instruction, for instance. We should boost efforts to repair the reading and mathematical deficits stemming from the COVID lockdowns. And we should launch informational campaigns to convince parents to spend more time reading to their children instead of sitting them in front of screens.

Wouldn’t it be wonderful to see the so-called rock stars of the Democratic Party push these changes? 

Presidential hopefuls Alexandria Ocasio Cortez and, ahem, Kamala Harris, could push for reforms and a return to the high educational standards of the past. They already talk about race incessantly. Maybe they could do so in a way that would benefit black and brown children?

Barack and Michelle Obama also could platform this idea on their podcasts and at their newly opened presidential library. 

Hillary “it takes a village” Clinton could call for educational excellence, as well.

If Democrats and others really want to see improvement in the numbers of black and brown doctors, they will start with the youngest children. 

Medical school is too late.

Stacy Washington is a decorated Air Force veteran, an Emmy-nominated TV personality and the host of “Stacy on the Right,” which airs nightly 9 p.m. to midnight ET on SiriusXM Patriot Channel 125.
news.summary: So you want diversity in California's top med schools?
news.author: Stacy Washington

news.title: Ed Power on Charlie's XCX not entirely successful revinvention
news.text:
The summer of 2025 was a tale of two concerts for Charli XCX, the English pop star whose Brat “era” ushered in a dizzying spell of overnight success for the former teenage raver from Essex and which, for a few fleeting moments, even threatened to change the course of the US presidential election.

On June16 she appeared at Malahide Castle in Dublin, where she clocked in with a headline set of just over an hour and where, singing from behind her sunglasses, she never looked more than vaguely engaged with her surroundings.

Charli’s biggest ever Irish show should have been a coronation. Instead it felt like a victory lap too far after 12 months in the spotlight, during which her Brat campaign had been co-opted by US Presidential candidate Kamala Harris (delivering a huge, if ultimately futile, boost to Harris’ street cred). The Brat era had also seen Charli ascend to the a-list by contributing to the soundtrack of the Barbie movie and following in the footsteps of Taylor Swift and Harry Styles by hosting Saturday Night Live. From out of the lime-green colour scheme of the Brat campaign a star had been born.

But at what price? It wasn’t that Charli could not be bothered in Dublin. The singer born Charlotte Aitchison has always been a hard worker - a diligence that continues with the release this month of her latest album Music, Fashion, Film. Still, the breakthrough of Brat and singles such as Von Dutch and Apple - with its accompanying viral TikTok dance - had obviously been a lot to deal with. In Dublin it was all too apparent that months of non-stop touring were taking their toll. This was a pop luminary in need of a holiday.

Twelve days later, she was back on stage at the UK’s Glastonbury festival, where she performed more or less the same set as the one she had delivered at Malahide. The biggest change arrived halfway through, when she set fire to a giant banner, “Brat”. The message was not subtle .When she released her biggest album in June 2024, Charli heralded the arrival of a “Brat Summer”. A year later she was burning it all down - ending Brat before Brat ended her.

Brat’s breakout had caught many in the music industry unawares. Up to that point, Charli was a big cult artist - capable of filling Dublin’s Olympia, as she did in May 2022, but hardly a megastar. “Brat was a bit of a surprise to us how well it sold, said Ray O’Brien of Cork record store Music Zone. “This album really crossed over to a lot of customers here in the shop. It is chaotic sounding at times....electro-pop fused with rave but really works.”

As the “Brat” logo burned amid the green swards of Glastonbury, it seemed Charli had seen the wood from the trees. Not every musician is built for success. For every Taylor Swift or Harry Styles there are artists for whom the bright lights and constant attention are not a gateway to pop heaven but a trapdoor to somewhere dark and dangerous.

Charli is quite clearly not in the same category as other more tragic figures, but in the run-up to Music, Fashion, Film she has been upfront about the pressures she has had to navigate post-Brat. “I recently have been really struggling with my mental health to the point where, if I’m being real, I’m in the worst place mentally that I’ve been in my life,” she told Rolling Stone in June.

The degree to which the LP will wrestle with these issues as yet unclear given that few have heard the album in its entirety. But the cover alone confirms she has moved on from the riotous lime green aesthetic of the Brat period. In place of that splurge of maximalism, the cover is a monochrome photograph of three of her favourite artists - former Velvet Underground guitarist John Cale, designer Marc Jacobs and director Martin Scorsese. Or, if you prefer, Music, Fashion, Film. 

Nor do we know whether she will address the fall-out from her rumoured rift with Taylor Swift, whom she supported at Croke Park, Dublin in 2018. While neither party has publicly commented on the falling-out, Charli is widely understood to have been the target of Swift’s stone-cold hit job, Isn’t It Romantic?, on her Life of a Showgirl LP.

“Wrote me a song saying it makes you sick to see my face,” sang Swift in a fake-chirpy tone that suggested revenge is a dish best served straight from the chiller. The internet has interpreted this as a reference to Brat track, Sympathy is a Knife, which was written around the time that Swift was dating The 1975’s Matty Healy - whose bandmate, George Daniel, was Charli’s then-boyfriend (now husband). Charli’s lyrics certainly leave you hanging on for more. “Don’t wanna see her backstage at my boyfriend’s show,” she sang. “Fingers crossed behind my back, I hope they break up quick.”

To be dragged into an internet feud is the last thing she will have wanted. But online tittle-tattle is alas part of the deal of being a pop star in 2026. No matter what you say or do, conspiracy theories will follow. That said, Charli XCX has also inspired more high-minded conversations, with academics variously heralding her lyric writing as high art and describing her as “Gen Z’s answer to the Romantic poets”. 

She has also become highly sought after by filmmakers - see, for instance, director Emerald Fennell who approached Charli asking if she would write a song for her adaptation of Wuthering Heights. Charli said yes and ended up composing the entire soundtrack. Charli found time, too, to release her own film - the satirical documentary film, The Moment, in which she pokes fun at the music industry and demonstrates her skills as an actor playing a heightened version of herself. 

The Moment was a curious project in that it portrays pop stardom as a sort of living hell - a weird flex from an artist who had only just achieved global fame after years of knocking on the door. Some, however, see her entire career as a commentary on celebrity and how fake it all is. 

“Her aim is to work across the mediums of music and film, mobilising this ‘tell all’ confessional mode in order to subvert the forms of performative ‘authenticity’ upon which twenty-first century celebrity relies,” says Dr Alice Pember, Assistant Professor of Film and Television Studies, University of Warwick (and Charli XCX fan) in an email.

She points to Charli’s recent single Rock Music - the first track released from Music, Fashion, Film - as an example of that philosophy in action. The tune is a riot of grungy post-punk guitars, over which Charli sings “I think the dance floor is dead, so no we’re making rock music”. But it does not entirely rip up the old Charli formula: she is still singing through Autotune, conjuring that familiar “warbling Dalek” effect. She is, in other words, messing with our heads slightly, asking us to consider whether music truly is more “real” just because it has guitars on it - a belief fervently held by many dyed-in-the-denim rock fans.

 “In musical terms, the turn to guitar music is something I’d point to here about authenticity and the way she plays with this musically,” says Pember. “Absolutely Charli always does this through autotune, which disrupts her ‘authentic’ voice etc but guitar rock is associated with qualities like rawness, so the level of polish in these recent releases at a musical level shows that she’s continuing to disrupt the line between reality and artifice through her sounds as well as film output.”

Brat and its bright green success came out of the blue for Charli, who released her debut studio album in 2013 and, up to two years ago, was perhaps best known for guesting on Iggy Azalea’s 2014 smash Fancy. What changed? The difference was that Charli had embraced her full, chaotic self with Brat - giving audiences a glimpse of the person behind the music. 

“Her personality is so infectious and it’s so relatable, even when she’s talking about doing something wrong or messy,” her former collaborator Danny Harle told me earlier this year. “She’s so articulate. [Brat] expresses such a combination of ideas that are emotionally intelligent and also just really satisfying. It’s the rawness of it. It is a truly holistic product. She was as much a part of the album doing well - in interviews, she was embodying the project. It was a funny combination of her stopping trying to make sort of pop hits and being the pop hit.”

But is she still “the pop hit”? Or has she gone back to being a cult artist? One thing that seems clear is that there will be no more Brat summers. A year after Malahide and Glastonbury, Charli is a very different pop star - an artist who has experienced success and also been hollowed out by it slightly. The queen of green has ditched the day-glo. With Music, Fashion, Film she has ripped up the script and is starring in an altogether different movie.

Music, Fashion, Film by Charli XCX is released July 24

news.title: Republican Hilton kicks off campaign for California governor
news.text:
LOS ANGELES (AP) — Republican Steve Hilton launched his general election campaign for California governor Wednesday with a gambit to win over left-leaning voters who dominate the state: Even if they loathe President Donald Trump, he said, they should consider voting for him.

During an event in Los Angeles, against the backdrop of a stinking, scorched food warehouse he described as a symbol of the state’s political incompetence, Hilton rolled out a list of campaign promises clearly aimed at residents buckling under California’s notoriously high cost of living. He vowed to bring the price of gasoline down to $3 a gallon, about half the current cost, cut residents’ utility bills in half, now among the nation’s highest, and eliminate state income taxes on the first $150,000 in earnings. 

He blamed the state’s problems — from homelessness to low student test scores — squarely on Democrats, who hold every statewide office and supermajorities in the Legislature. He called Democrats a “rotten regime that has abandoned the working class.”

In his campaign remarks and ads he took out in leading newspapers throughout the state, Hilton addressed his relationship with Trump, who has endorsed him in the race.

“This is not about who you hate in Washington. It’s about what we love about California and what we can do to make California once again the best place anywhere in the country to start and raise a family, to start and run a business,” he said before a small group of supporters. “That’s what this election is about.”

Hilton’s maneuver is meant to create a sliver of distance from Trump without breaking from him. It’s a tacit nod to the political math in California, one of the nation’s most solidly Democratic states. Registered Republicans are outnumbered nearly 2-to-1 by Democratic voters, and a GOP candidate hasn’t won a statewide election since 2006.

Trump is widely unpopular in California outside his loyal conservative base. In 2016, Trump lost to Democrat Hillary Clinton by 30 points; in 2020, he lost to Joe Biden by 29 points; and in 2024, he lost to Kamala Harris by 20 points. 

California is regarded as the home of the so-called Trump resistance, where Democrats have effectively used Trump as a foil in campaigns to motivate voters. Last year, Gov. Gavin Newsom essentially nationalized the state’s fight over Proposition 50, which recast U.S. House districts to favor Democrats, by framing it as a confrontation with Trump. The measure notched a 29-point win.

The campaign of Hilton’s Democratic opponent, former U.S. Health and Human Services Secretary Xavier Becerra, scoffed at the notion of Hilton being a change agent.

“Californians already know who Steve Hilton is: Trump’s puppet in California. His campaign is a promise to run our state the way Trump runs the country and we’re not buying it,” campaign spokesman Jonathan Underland said in a statement.

As for Hilton’s pitch that voters who dislike Trump should feel free to consider him in November, Underland said, “Steve Hilton cheers on a president whose illegal war has sent gas prices soaring and illegal tariffs have cost California families more than $2,500 this year alone. You can’t fix the affordability crisis while carrying water for the man causing it.”

In a heavily Democratic state, Hilton’s campaign is hoping his appeal to Democrats and independents will “give voters a permission slip” to look past the letter next to a candidate’s name, even in a nation deeply divided by partisanship.

“You do have a choice if you think that this state is going in the wrong direction,” he told supporters at a kickoff event in Boyle Heights, a Los Angeles neighborhood where residents have been complaining about the stench of rotting food, flies and rodents that followed a fire last month at a cold storage warehouse.

He was accompanied by Gloria Romero, a former Democratic state lawmaker who switched parties in 2024 and is this year’s Republican nominee for lieutenant governor. She delivered her own address in both English and Spanish, with supporters holding “Hilton-Romero” signs in the background.

Becerra, a former state lawmaker, congressman and state attorney general, is heavily favored to win the seat now held by Newsom, a likely 2028 Democratic presidential candidate who is barred by law from seeking a third term. Hilton and Becerra emerged from a chaotic primary election in June that had more than 50 candidates on the ballot.

The race is unfolding at a time when California is facing multiple crises. Despite billions in spending, homelessness remains widespread, especially in larger cities. Multibillion-dollar budget gaps are projected into the future. Home prices remain out of reach for many residents. The home insurance market is in disarray, businesses and residents have been fleeing the state, and residents contend with some of the nation’s highest gas prices, taxes and utility bills.

Making good on his campaign promises won’t be an easy task for Hilton, a former adviser to ex-United Kingdom Prime Minister David Cameron who has never held elective office. The governor doesn’t control the oil or utility markets, but Hilton says he will get rid of mandates, taxes and fees that drive those prices higher. He also would likely need the consent of a Legislature controlled by Democrats to change the state’s income tax structure.

Hilton crowned his campaign kickoff with a 30-second TV ad underscoring the financial challenges for many families.

“Life here has become impossible for anyone who isn’t already rich,” he says. “Let’s fix California.”
news.author: Michael R. Blood

news.title: 1 in 5 Americans say communism a ‘major threat’ to US, bombshell poll finds
news.text:
Red-Dem-ption.

Just 1 in 5 Americans believe communism poses a “major threat” in the US, a bombshell new poll has found.

And as the 2028 Presidential Election approaches, a significant number of voters see two of the most high-profile potential Democratic candidates as “socialists.”

Two-thirds (66%) of Americans believe communists pose at least some threat to the nation, according to a poll published Tuesday by the Economist and YouGov.

Some 18% see communists as a major threat, 25% see them as a moderate threat, and 23% see them as a minor threat.

There were stark differences of opinion between liberal and conservative Americans on the danger of communism.

Among those who self-described as very liberal or liberal, 79% and 52% respectively said that communists were not a threat.

In contrast, 26% of conservatives and 52% of the very conservative said communists represent a major threat in America today.

The survey also asked Americans to rank politicians based on whether they thought they were socialist.

One in 5 (20%) would classify Gavin Newsom as a socialist and more than a quarter (27%) would classify Kamala Harris as a socialist.

New York’s Mayor Zohran Mamdani was viewed as a socialist by 51% of the adult population, just shy of the 55% who think the same of veteran Vermont Senator and would-be presidential candidate Bernie Sanders.

Almost half (45%) of those surveyed said they believed New York Congresswoman Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez was a socialist.

Elizabeth Warren finished out the list, with 25% of Americans believing the Massachusetts Senator to be a socialist.

Of the other prominent Democrats listed in the survey, all of them are considered socialist by at least one in five voters.

Almost a quarter (23%) view former President Joe Biden as a socialist, just slightly less than the 27% who think so of his former boss, President Barack Obama.

Those with different political views also had different verdicts on how left-wing leading Democrats were.

For instance, only 6% of people who identify as very liberal said they believe Gov. Newsom is a socialist, but 52% of those who are very conservative identify him as a socialist.
news.summary: Some 18% see communists as a major threat, 25% see them as a moderate threat, and 23% see them as a minor threat.
news.author: Anthony Blair

news.title: MS NOW, Sharpton Compare 2016 Trump Election to 1898 White Supremacist Coup
news.text:
Appearing as a guest on Tuesday's Morning Joe, New Yorker staff writer Lauren Collins joined show regular Al Sharpton in comparing the election of Donald Trump in 2016 to an 1898 coup by white supremacists in Wilmington, North Carolina.

Host Joe Scarborough began the segment by recalling the violent episode written about in Collins's book, They Stole a City. Here's Scarborough:

A groundbreaking new book is shedding light on what's thought to be the only successful coup in U.S. history. In They Stole a City, our next guest chronicles the city of Wilmington, North Carolina, in 1898, then considered a bastion of black success with a black middle class brimming with lawyers, educators, and elected officials. But in November of that year, white supremacists issued a white declaration of independence, gunning down scores of black men while chasing their families into hiding. They then marched to city hall and overthrew the democratically elected multiracial government at gunpoint.

After Collins recalled how she became interested in the topic, and the basics of what happened, Sharpton invoked Trump in a follow-up question:

Lauren, talk about how there is the underlying principles of some of that when they see black advancement coming out of slavery, Reconstruction era, that they never lose the feeling of white supremacy that we are supposed to be in charge. And that's the underlying feeling that led to what happened in Wilmington. And some of us feeling that, in a less violent way, it has happened in modern times -- in our times of where you had a Barack Obama as President, black making advancement, and saw that a lot of the reaction was Donald Trump and a backlash, or even Donald Trump and a backlash to Kamala Harris. Talk about how that kind of underlying feeling may be the core of what we saw in Wilmington and still see some segments of that now.

Collins began her response:

Absolutely. I mean, we have to remember, this was only 30 years after slavery and emancipation. These people should have been celebrated for what they've done -- what they had done. It was a huge achievement. But instead it did it -- Wilmington is a case study, a flagrant case study of this pattern of white backlash to black political process -- progress. So pursue reconstruction, you get 1898 -- pursue desegregation, you're met with massive resistance -- elect a black President, endure a white nationalist one who claims that his predecessor was not a citizen.

Transcript follows:

MS NOW's Morning Joe

July 21, 2026

6:47 a.m. Eastern

JOE SCARBOROUGH: A groundbreaking new book is shedding light on what's thought to be the only successful coup in U.S. history. In They Stole a City, our next guest chronicles the city of Wilmington, North Carolina, in 1898, then considered a bastion of black success with a black middle class brimming with lawyers, educators, and elected officials. But in November of that year, white supremacists issued a white declaration of independence, gunning down scores of black men while chasing their families into hiding. They then marched to city hall and overthrew the democratically elected multiracial government at gunpoint.

Nearly 130 years later, the new book weaves together the stories of four Wilmington families who were present at the time, two white families who contributed to the violence, and two black families who survived it. And the author of that book, Lauren Collins, joins us now. She's a staff writer at the New Yorker, and Wilmington, North Carolina, is her home town.

Thank you so much for being with us. You know, I think it was about a year ago that a lot of Americans who had never heard about the Tulsa race riots, never been taught it in school, heard about all the horrors of that. I think you could say the same thing about what happened in 1898 in your hometown. Talk about it. Talk about why it was so significant and why you wrote the book.

6:51 a.m. Eastern

AL SHARPTON: Lauren, talk about how there is the underlying principles of some of that when they see black advancement coming out of slavery, Reconstruction era, that they never lose the feeling of white supremacy that we are supposed to be in charge. And that's the underlying feeling that led to what happened in Wilmington. And some of us feeling that, in a less violent way, it has happened in modern times -- in our times of where you had a Barack Obama as President, black making advancement, and saw that a lot of the reaction was Donald Trump and a backlash, or even Donald Trump and a backlash to Kamala Harris. Talk about how that kind of underlying feeling may be the core of what we saw in Wilmington and still see some segments of that now.

LAUREN COLLINS, THE NEW YORKER: Absolutely. I mean, we have to remember, this was only 30 years after slavery and emancipation. These people should have been celebrated for what they've done -- what they had done. It was a huge achievement. But instead it did it -- Wilmington is a case study, a flagrant case study of this pattern of white backlash to black political process -- progress. So pursue reconstruction, you get 1898 -- pursue desegregation, you're met with massive resistance -- elect a black President, endure a white nationalist one who claims that his predecessor was not a citizen.

I think it's very important to keep in mind that white supremacy is not a blip. It's not a regression. It's a through line of our nation's history. And what Wilmington and the story of 1898 demonstrates is that white supremacists will continue to take what they think is theirs until the costs outweigh the spoils. You know, I think it's very important to keep in mind both the Department of Homeland Security and the FBI have designated white supremacy as the most persistent and lethal terrorist threat in America. So, absolutely, this is living history. The story is not over. And I think the origins of many of our current crises are here, right, you know, can be seen in our own violent past.

news.title: Marco Rubio Surging as JD Vance Drops Double Digits in New 2028 Poll
news.text: A newly released University of New Hampshire Survey Center poll suggests the early Republican race for the 2028 presidential nomination may be tightening, with Secretary of State Marco Rubio posting significant gains while Vice President JD Vance's once-commanding lead has narrowed. The survey found Vance still leading the GOP field in New Hampshire, but Rubio has surged into a much stronger second-place position, trimming what had previously been a large double-digit advantage. The results seemingly indicate that Republican voters are beginning to take a closer look at multiple potential successors to President Donald Trump as the next White House contest slowly comes into focus. Rubio's rise marks one of the biggest shifts from earlier polling, where Vance consistently enjoyed a commanding lead over the rest of the Republican field. The latest numbers suggest Rubio's growing national profile and prominent role in the Trump administration may be resonating with Granite State voters. While Vance remains the favorite, the shrinking margin could signal a more competitive Republican primary if both men ultimately decide to run. No candidate has formally announced their candidacy. What To Know According to the poll, Vance has 36 percent of the potential vote compared to Rubio's 26 percent. Eight percent would vote for either Kentucky Senator Rand Paul or former UN Ambassador Nikki Haley, 6 percent would pick Florida Governor Ron DeSantis, 4 percent would vote for former Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard, and 2 percent chose Louisiana Senator John Kennedy. Other candidates landed less than 2 percent in the poll. An earlier survey in February by the pollster showed Vance landed 53 percent compared to Rubio's 7 percent. In a poll taken in October of last year, Vance had 51 percent compared to Rubio's 5 percent. Grant Reeher, a professor of political science at Syracuse University, told Newsweek via email on Wednesday, "Rubio’s surge doesn’t surprise me. He has been more in the news lately, given the U.S. international posture, and he has managed to sound reasonable and grounded, in contrast to the defense secretary, for instance." Reeher later added, "Vance will be perceived as the more Trump-like candidate, given his proximity to Trump, and some of his rhetoric. If they run a full primary, and Trump doesn’t put his thumb—or fist, rather—on the scale, I would expect Rubio to prevail. He will tap some of the buyer’s remorse over Trump, which is evident in some of the polling, and among the Party’s elite. And he is also the more strategic candidate in the general election—he could dampen some of the deep resentment toward Trump that Vance would activate." On the Democratic side, the poll shows Representative Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez and former Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg leading the field, highlighting a potential emerging divide between the party's progressive and moderate wings. Both candidates outpaced other potential Democratic contenders in the early survey, underscoring their continued national visibility despite neither having formally launched a presidential campaign. With nearly two years before the first presidential primaries, the Democratic race remains highly fluid. Wednesday's poll shows that Ocasio-Cortez has 22 percent compared to Buttigieg's 21 percent. Arizona Senator Mark Kelly landed 9 percent, California Governor Gavin Newsom has 8 percent, Vermont Senator Bernie Sanders has 6 percent, and former Vice President Kamala Harris has 5 percent. No other candidate obtained 5 percent or more in the poll. The poll surveyed 648 likely 2028 Democratic presidential primary voters with a margin of error of 3.8 percent and 579 likely 2028 Republican presidential primary voters with a margin of error of 4.1 percent from July 15 to July 20, 2026, the poll says. The findings also stand in contrast to earlier national surveys this year that showed Vance holding a double-digit advantage over other prospective Republican candidates. While national polling has previously placed the vice president comfortably ahead, New Hampshire voters appear to be giving Rubio fresh momentum as speculation surrounding the 2028 race intensifies. Political observers caution that early-state polls remain snapshots rather than predictions, but the latest survey shows that the Republican nomination contest may be becoming more competitive. What Other Polls Show A Big Data Poll last month found Vance leading prospective Republican contenders with 35.4 percent support, more than double that of Rubio, who finished second with 16.5 percent. On the Democratic side, Harris led the field with 29.1 percent, ahead of Newsom with 15 percent. The poll was taken from June 26 to June 28, 2026, among 1,261 registered Republican voters and has a 1.8 percent margin of error. A Zogby Analytics poll released this month shows Vance leading Ocasio-Cortez and Newsom in hypothetical 2028 presidential matchups among likely U.S. voters. Vance leads Ocasio-Cortez with 45 percent compared to 37.6 percent in a potential matchup and leads Newsom with 45.1 percent compared to 38.1 percent. The poll surveyed 1,003 likely voters from July 1 to July 4, 2026, with a margin of error of 3.1 percent. What Has JD Vance Said About a Potential 2028 Run? Vance has repeatedly declined to formally commit to a 2028 presidential campaign, saying his focus is on serving in the current administration rather than planning his next political move. "Yes, after the midterms, I will eventually have to make that decision," he told Megyn Kelly last month in part, amid a wide-ranging interview. Speaking to CBS Sunday Morning last month alongside Second Lady Usha Vance, JD said in part, "I have no doubt that the president of the United States is going to be very supportive of anything that I ultimately decide to do." He added, "But we really just haven't talked about what that thing will be." In May when asked about Trump’s previous comments calling a possible Vance-Rubio ticket a "dream team," Vance told Newsweek during a news conference at the White House that Americans would likely unfavorably view “someone who’s barely been in one office for a year and a half angling for a job two and a half years down the road,” adding, “There are few topics I want to talk about less.” Contact Newsweek editors on this story: Gray R. Thomas
news.summary: The latest survey shows that the Republican nomination contest may be becoming more competitive.
news.author: Anna Commander

news.title: Fox News Poll: Voters want major change amid economic and political discontent
news.text: Several findings in the latest Fox News Poll show voters are unhappy and want major change, which bodes well for Democrats in November: President Donald Trump’s job approval remains near a record low, more Democrats are motivated to vote, Republicans join Democrats and independents in rating the economy negatively, and 4 in 10 voters want "dramatic" or "revolutionary" change. Plus, more view the GOP as tied to elites than the Democratic Party. Voters say Democrats can better handle income inequality (+22 points), healthcare (+21), inflation (+10), the economy (+9), and foreign policy (+7). Republicans hold the advantage on crime (+7 points), while government spending (Democrats +3) and national security (R +2) are more evenly divided. On immigration, the GOP had an 8-point edge in April. Now, Democrats are preferred by one point. FOX NEWS POLL: AN EARLY LOOK AT THE GEORGIA SENATE RACE More Democrats (68%) say they are extremely motivated to vote in November than Republicans (57%) by an 11-point margin. By a 25-point spread, more MAGA Republicans (66%) are extremely motivated than non-MAGA Republicans (41%). FOX NEWS POLL: A CLOSE SENATE CONTEST IS BREWING IN IOWA If the election were today, 53% of voters would back the Democratic candidate in their House district and 46% the Republican. That 7-point edge is just outside the poll’s margin of error. Democrats were up by 5 points in April. Notably, 53% is the highest percentage either party has received on the generic House ballot in Fox News polling since 1996. When looking only at counties where the 2024 presidential vote was close — Trump and former Vice President Kamala Harris within 10 points — the Democratic candidate leads by 59-41%. "Although these data look promising for the Democrats, some of their support is in less-contested districts, padding their national totals without adding House seats," says Republican pollster Daron Shaw, who conducts Fox News polls with Democrat Chris Anderson. "The best estimates I’ve seen suggest the GOP could retain the House even if the Democrats win the national vote by 2-3 points, but if Democrats win by 6-7 points, they probably take the House." The Democratic advantage comes amid weak ratings for Trump and continued economic pessimism. Trump’s job approval stands at 39%, unchanged for the third month in a row and one point above his low of 38% in October 2017. Approval of Trump is a touch lower than former President Barack Obama’s at the same point in his second term (42% in July 2014). Trump received net-negative ratings for handling immigration (42% approve, 57% disapprove), Iran (34-66%), the economy (33-67%), and inflation (27-73%). Three-quarters rate the economy negatively, about the same for the past six months. Another three quarters say inflation has caused them financial hardship, including 34% who say "serious" hardship, up from 26% last year. Fifty-four percent expect the economy to worsen in the coming year; only Republicans express optimism (71%), while Democrats (75%) and independents (64%) expect it to worsen. Dissatisfaction extends beyond individual figures to both political parties and the broader economic system. "Democrats and Republicans have a surprisingly broad array of shared beliefs about politics today," says Shaw. "Both think elected officials answer to the elites, both think the system is rigged, both want to see new blood in office, both feel betrayed by their party, both disapprove of their members in Congress, and both want a mandatory retirement age for Congress." Some 70% say the Republican Party is closely tied to elite interests, while 55% say the same about the Democratic Party. The 15-point gap reflects more Democrats and independents saying the GOP is tied to elites, yet even Republicans are equally likely to describe both parties as close to elites. There’s disillusionment in both parties. Many Democrats (48%) and Republicans (41%) say they feel betrayed by their party, and for both it is primarily for selling out to special interests. Perceptions that the political system favors elites are reshaping views of the economy as well. The number who accept income inequality because "that’s how our economy works" has fallen 16 points compared to 2014 (from 62% to 46%), while the share who want government action has more than doubled (from 13% to 30%). At the same time, an increasing number, 65%, think the country’s system is rigged in favor of the wealthy (up 9 points compared to 2019). By 2-to-1, voters say it is more important to elect new people than keep those with experience. Overall, 19% say they want revolutionary change in the nation’s government, while many others want substantial (38%) or dramatic (23%) change. Almost twice as many Democrats (52%) as Republicans (28%) want dramatic or revolutionary change. Nearly 7 in 10 voters disapprove of both Democrats and Republicans in Congress. Some 32% of Republicans disapprove of their party’s lawmakers, while 39% of Democrats disapprove of theirs. More than 8 in 10 independents disapprove of both. Fully 85% support a mandatory retirement age for members of Congress, up 20 points since 2002. Of those in favor of setting a cap, 80% think it should be before age 75, with 61% selecting a cutoff between 65 and 74 — a boundary supported by more than half of every generation. Poll-pourri Just 50% of voters have a positive view of capitalism, down from a high of 57% in 2019. For socialism, it’s 30% favorable, up from 27% last year but below the 32% who said the same in 2021. The Democratic Socialists of America are viewed positively by 31%. By an 11-point margin, more Democrats have a favorable opinion of socialism (52%) than capitalism (41%), while by a 50-point margin, more Republicans have a positive view of capitalism (61%) than socialism (11%). Forty-five percent of voters have a favorable view of Vermont Sen. Bernie Sanders, I-Vt., That makes him more popular than Vice President JD Vance (41% favorable), Trump (38%), and Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, D-N.Y., (35%). Each is viewed more unfavorably than favorably. Voters under 30 and those ages 65+ are equally likely to view Sanders positively (48% and 46% respectively). For AOC, more voters 65+ (41%) have a favorable view than young voters (33%), and three times as many of those under 30 say they’ve never heard of her (30% vs. 9%). Overall, 44% have a favorable view of Israel, down from 58% a decade ago. Positive ratings are lower today among Democrats (by 14 points), Republicans (-12), and independents (-21). Voters ages 65 and over (56%) are twice as likely to view Israel favorably as those under 30 (27%). Conducted July 17–20, 2026, under the direction of Beacon Research (D) and Shaw & Company Research (R), this Fox News survey includes interviews with a sample of 1,003 registered voters randomly selected from a national voter file. Respondents spoke with live interviewers on landlines (70) and cellphones (681) or completed the survey online after receiving a text (252). Results based on the full sample have a margin of sampling error of ±3 percentage points. The survey includes an oversample of 200 voters aged 18–29, bringing the sample size for that subgroup to 350 (±5). Sampling error for results among subgroups is higher. In addition to sampling errors, question wording and order can influence results. Weights are generally applied to age, race, education, and area variables to ensure the demographics are representative of the registered voter population. Sources for developing weight targets include the most recent American Community Survey, Fox News Voter Analysis, and voter file data. Fox News’ Victoria Balara contributed to this report.
news.summary: Trump approval holds at 39% as Democrats lead the generic ballot by 7 points, the highest for either party in Fox News polling since 1996.
news.author: Dana Blanton

news.title: Extreme Candidates Have Cost Arizona Republicans a Bunch of Elections. Guess What the Plan Is for 2026?
news.text:
Geographically, Arizona is beautiful. Politically, it’s a little bewildering. The state, once a Republican stronghold associated with Barry Goldwater and John McCain, now has a more muddled image. GOP presidential candidates still win Arizona more often than not, and Republicans have controlled the Legislature for decades, proof that the party remains competitive all around this increasingly purple state. But in recent years, the GOP has fumbled away statewide races for governor, secretary of state, and U.S. Senate by nominating extreme candidates. And the events of last night suggest that Republicans may be about to get bitten again.

Back up. What happened last night?

Arizona held primary elections for governor, secretary of state, and U.S. House. On the Republican side, ultraconservatives dominated the marquee races. The GOP nominee for governor is Rep. Andy Biggs, who once led the House’s far-right Freedom Caucus and voted to overturn the 2020 election results. For secretary of state, Republicans went with Alexander Kolodin, a lawyer who was sanctioned by the state bar for parroting Donald Trump’s false claims that 2020 was stolen. With Trump’s endorsement, Biggs and Kolodin beat Republican rivals who argued that the eventual primary winners were both liable to lose a general election.

How do they compare to past GOP candidates in Arizona?

The Democrats whom Biggs and Kolodin will face in November—Gov. Katie Hobbs and Secretary of State Adrian Fontes—are arguably both in office today because Republicans nominated similarly extreme candidates last time. The GOP also picked election deniers for high-profile races in 2022: former TV newscaster Kari Lake for governor, militia member Mark Finchem for secretary of state, and the robotically uncharismatic Blake Masters for Senate. All three lost, even as Republicans held the state Legislature and picked up U.S. House seats. (Lake lost again in 2024, this time for a U.S. Senate seat, even as Trump won Arizona that year.)

Sounds like Republicans are about to rerun that experiment.

Trump celebrated last night’s results as “a complete sweep” for MAGA. Given the president’s deep unpopularity, Biggs and Kolodin could easily lose in a rout.

OK, but couldn’t they still win?

Indeed. Trump, the OG election denier, won Arizona twice and came very close in 2020. And if November turns out to be a Democratic ripple rather than a wave, we could end up with election deniers overseeing the 2028 presidential race in a key swing state.

[Shudders] Did the extremist pattern continue in other races?

You bet. Mark Lamb, a Trump-endorsed former sheriff who’s been accused of sexting outside his marriage and making racist jokes over text, will almost certainly inherit Biggs’ solidly Republican House seat. And Jay Feely, a former Arizona Cardinals kicker, won the primary for an open swing seat with Trump’s backing.

Did anything noteworthy happen on the Democratic side?

This year’s primaries have been a mixed bag for the Democratic establishment, and last night’s were no exception. Rep. Greg Stanton easily fended off a left-wing challenger. But in the race to take on Feely, Amish Shah, an emergency room physician, is leading Marlene Galán-Woods, a former broadcast journalist whom Democrats’ House campaign arm had endorsed. Shah, Democrats’ 2024 nominee in the district, got fewer votes there than Kamala Harris. Republicans, it may turn out, aren’t the only ones willing to risk fumbling a winnable election in Arizona.

OK, you’re caught up on a few races that could have national implications. To get your head out of Grand Canyon State politics and kick back tonight, I recommend: 

A primer for romance in the digital age: People talk a lot about how dating apps have made it harder to meet someone. But people rarely talk about how the internet has also changed what it’s like to be in a relationship. On ICYMI, host Kate Lindsay and Slate tech editor Tony Ho Tran break down the digital do’s and don’ts for couples, and argue that the thing that ruined dating might actually be making relationships better.

A satisfying puzzle break: What’s a four-letter word for “Those who often hear a siren song”? Treat your brain to some delightful wordplay in today’s crossword.

An old-school tip for finding new music: If Spotify’s algorithm makes you feel stuck in a musical rut, there’s an easy way out. Turn on the radio! It’s an underrated, retro way to discover songs you’ve never heard, songs by artists you love, and songs by artists you forgot you love.

Some summer advice: Going for a dip is a relaxing way to beat the heat, but occasionally, something goes wrong. Whether you have questions about personal grooming for bikini season or saving seats while you’re swimming, Slate’s advice columnists have suggestions for making sure nothing goes awry on your next trip to the pool or the beach.

We hope all this helps you have a great rest of your evening. See you tomorrow!
news.summary: The new nominee for governor is a 2020 election denier—and so much more.
news.author: Ian Prasad Philbrick

news.title: AOC surges past Buttigieg, leaves Harris in the dust in key 2028 battleground poll
news.text: Progressive firebrand Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez has surged to the front of the prospective 2028 Democratic presidential field in New Hampshire, edging Pete Buttigieg and leaving Kamala Harris far behind in a new poll. Ocasio-Cortez drew 22% support among likely Democratic primary voters in the University of New Hampshire Granite State Poll released Wednesday, one point ahead of former Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg and 17 points ahead of former Vice President Kamala Harris. Ocasio-Cortez gained seven points since the February survey, while Harris and California Gov. Gavin Newsom lost ground — another sign of the New York lawmaker’s growing strength among Democratic voters in the early presidential battleground. MIDTERM BATTLE FOR THE HOUSE: CHECK OUT THE FOX NEWS POWER RANKINGS Ocasio-Cortez has been coy about her 2028 plans and careful not to rule out either a White House run in the race to succeed term-limited President Donald Trump, or a bid for the Senate against longtime Democratic Leader Sen. Chuck Schumer, who is up for re-election in two years. When asked about a run for higher office, Ocasio-Cortez recently said her goal is to "change the country." Buttigieg, a moderate who as the former mayor of South Bend, Indiana came in second in the 2020 New Hampshire presidential primary, has crisscrossed the country in support of fellow Democrats the past year as speculation builds around a possible 2028 run. "Nearly half of socialists and one-third of progressives support Ocasio-Cortez, while pluralities of liberals and moderates support Buttigieg," Andrew Smith, the director of the University of New Hampshire Survey Center, said. KAMALA HARRIS' TRAVELS AND COMMENTS CLEARLY POINT TO A 2028 RUN Sen. Mark Kelly of Arizona, a retired astronaut, stood in third place in the poll, at nine percent, with term-limited Newsom a point back. Newsom's support dropped seven points since the February survey. Independent Sen. Bernie Sanders of Vermont, the 2016 and 2020 runner-up in the Democratic presidential primaries, stood at six percent. The 84-year-old Sanders, asked about another White House run, said last month, "I suspect that's not going to happen." He jokingly added, "I know I look like I'm 30. I am not." Meanwhile, Harris, the party's 2024 nominee after she replaced then-President Joe Biden, who dropped out less than four months before the election following a disastrous debate against Trump, grabbed only 5% support in the new poll, down from 10% in February. While the former vice president's numbers in the new New Hampshire survey are nothing to brag about, she tops an average of the latest national polls in the hypothetical race for the 2028 Democratic nomination. Surveys in the early stages of a presidential cycle are often heavily influenced by the name recognition of the potential candidates. DEMOCRACY ’26: STAY UP TO DATE WITH THE FOX NEWS ELECTION HUB Everyone else in the large field of potential contenders tested in the new New Hampshire poll registered at two percent or less. New Hampshire held the first-in-the-nation presidential primary for a century, before Democrats upended their nominating calendar in the 2024 cycle, moving South Carolina to the lead-off position. The Democratic National Committee's (DNC) Rules and Bylaws panel is likely on Friday to unveil a new calendar for the 2028 cycle, which would be voted on at the DNC's summer meeting in Austin, Texas next month. Republicans have not altered their calendar, keeping the Iowa caucuses as their lead-off state, followed by New Hampshire. In the race for the GOP presidential nomination, 36% of likely Republican primary voters in New Hampshire said they would support Vice President JD Vance, with Secretary of State Marco Rubio at 26%. SIGN UP TO GET THE POLITICS NEWSLETTER Sen. Rand Paul of Kentucky, who ran for the nomination in 2016 and is mulling another bid, stood at eight percent in the survey, along with former ambassador and former South Carolina Gov. Nikki Haley, who was the runner-up to Trump in 2024. CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD THE FOX NEWS APP Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis, who also bid for the nomination in 2024, grabbed six percent support, with everyone else tested at 4% or less.
news.summary: Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez surged to 22% in the New Hampshire poll, edging Pete Buttigieg by one point as the 2028 Democratic primary field takes shape.
news.author: Paul Steinhauser

news.title: AOC picks up steam while Vance loses ground to Rubio: New Hampshire poll
news.text:
Arizona Sen. Mark Kelly (D-AZ) followed with 9%, California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D-CA) received 8%, Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-VT) drew 6%, and former Vice President Kamala Harris garnered 5%. No other potential candidate reached more than 2%.

Support for Ocasio-Cortez has increased seven points since February, while Newsom’s support fell 7 points, and Harris dropped 5 points. The poll found Ocasio-Cortez’s backing comes largely from self-described socialists and progressives.

Buttigieg, by contrast, draws support more evenly from progressives, liberals, and moderates. Kelly performs best among liberals, while Newsom’s support is concentrated among moderates.

Supporters of Ocasio-Cortez most frequently cited her policies and political ideology as the reason for backing her. Buttigieg and Kelly supporters most often pointed to their qualifications and competence.

Despite growing speculation about a White House bid, Ocasio-Cortez has repeatedly declined to say whether she plans to run.

“My ambition is to change this country,” the congresswoman said in May when asked about a possible 2028 campaign. “Presidents come and go. Senate, House seats, elected officials come and go. But single-payer healthcare is forever.”

Rubio cuts into Vance’s Republican lead

On the Republican side, Vance remains the frontrunner, but Rubio has made substantial gains over the past several months.

Among likely Republican primary voters, 36% said they would back Vance, while 26% chose Rubio. Sen. Rand Paul (R-KY) and former United Nations Ambassador Nikki Haley each received 8%, followed by Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis at 6%, former Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard at 4%, and Sen. John Kennedy (R-LA) at 2%.

Rubio’s support has jumped 19 points since February, while Vance’s has declined 17 points over the same period. The poll found Vance’s support is strongest among conservatives, while Rubio performs better with moderates and libertarians.

About two-thirds of Republican voters said they want the party’s 2028 nominee to continue President Donald Trump’s agenda rather than chart a different course. However, fewer than half said a Trump endorsement would make them more likely to support a candidate.

Republican voters often cited candidates’ policies, experience, and qualifications as the reasons for their support. Vance supporters most frequently pointed to his qualifications, values, and experience, while Rubio supporters emphasized his experience and competence.

Other candidates struggle

Several Democrats frequently mentioned as possible 2028 contenders failed to register meaningful support in the poll.

Gov. Josh Shapiro (D-PA), Rep. Ro Khanna (D-CA), Sen. Cory Booker (D-NJ), Gov. Andy Beshear (D-KY), and Gov. J.B. Pritzker (D-IL) all remained in the single digits.

Pritzker has repeatedly downplayed speculation that he plans to seek the presidency.

“Not at all,” Pritzker said when asked if he was preparing a White House campaign. “I literally, I’m running for reelection as governor because we have a whole lot more work to do in the state.”

Still, he has stopped short of ruling out a future bid. Last year, Pritzker told NBC’s Meet the Press that he “can’t rule anything out,” and in June said he expects to play a significant role in the 2028 election.

BEN SHAPIRO SAYS VANCE’S APPEARANCE ON ROGAN PUTS HIM IN A WORSE SPOT FOR 2028

Other Democrats, including Newsom and former Chicago Mayor Rahm Emanuel, have fueled speculation by increasing their national profiles, though many party leaders remain focused on the 2026 midterm elections before making presidential decisions.

The Granite State Poll was conducted by the University of New Hampshire Survey Center from July 15 to 20. Researchers surveyed 1,458 New Hampshire residents online, including members of the Granite State Panel and respondents recruited by text message. The survey has an overall margin of error of plus or minus 2.6 percentage points.
news.summary: Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-NY) leads the crowded field of potential Democratic presidential contenders among New Hampshire voters, while Secretary of State Marco Rubio has narrowed Vice President JD Vance’s lead in the Republican race, according to a new poll. The recent poll surveyed likely 2028 presidential primary voters in New Hampshire between July 15 and […]
news.author: Rena Rowe

news.title: Kamala Harris' White House Dreams Crushed: Former Biden Aide Insists Ex-Veep Has 'No Chance' of Winning 2028 Presidential Election
news.text:
Kamala Harris hasn't yet announced a plan to run for president again – and an aide close to her claimed it may be for the better that way.

Michael LaRosa, former First Lady Jill Biden’s press secretary, isn't confident Harris, 61, is capable of winning a 2028 election, RadarOnline.com has learned.Ex-Biden Staffer Claims Kamala Harris Has No ChanceThe ex-staffer told Fox News' Gillian Turner that Harris has no chance. According to LaRosa, Harris has been in public service for too long, meaning people are losing interest in her. 

He said, "Nobody wants to – people want to hear what she has to say, and the problem is people want that new car smell, as Barack Obama once said. They are not going to give Kamala Harris a third at bat."

LaRosa isn't confident Harris would even be able to secure the Democratic nomination. Strategically, he argued, Rahm Emanuel, Gavin Newsom or Pete Buttigieg would be better candidates. 

"There is no appetite for another Kamala Harris candidacy," he added, suggesting consultants around her likely already know.Harris' Leadership Under QuestionHe further criticized Harris' leadership, arguing she's more of a follower. 

LaRosa added, "There’s a lot of people who are qualified and will be not only good candidates but good stewards of the office. I think one of the problems is that Harris, like I said, she doesn’t lead, she follows."

He predicted Harris is keeping tabs on the upcoming midterm election, using the voters' choices as an indicator. However, plans to "ride the wave" is exactly what LaRosa believes is wrong with Harris.

He added, "She doesn’t lead. She follows. She doesn’t take risks. She calculates. She doesn’t answer questions, she equivocates. She’s got to get over that if she wants to make a connection with people but I don’t think she’s going to get a third at bat."Harris Ran Twice So FarHarris has ran for president two times. In the 2020 election, she was an early candidate for the primary, but withdrew her candidacy in December 2019 due to a lack of funds.

After serving as former President Joe Biden's vice president for a term, she rejoined him on the campaign trail for the 2024 election.

While she did not enter the election cycle as a candidate, she did ultimately become the Democratic nominee. The party initially named Joe as their candidate to run against Donald Trump. However, concerns over his cognitive ability and age forced Joe, 83, to step aside.

When he decided to exit the election, Harris stepped up to fill his position. She named Tim Walz as her own running mate.

The late start to campaigning did her no favors at the polls, and many voters were frustrated that she was not the direct winner of the primary. In the end, Harris lost the election to Trump, 80.'I Am Thinking About It'Harris hasn't yet indicated a plan to run for president again. Following her defeat, she took time off to be closer to family and friends. She did, however, admit it wasn't totally out of the question.

"I might. I am thinking about it," Harris told Rev. Al Sharpton in April 2026.
news.summary: A former aide to the Biden family claimed Kamala Harris won't be able to win a presidential election in 2028.
news.author: Alex West

news.title: Four Republicans Side with Democrats to Kill NDAA Amendment Banning Trans-Identifying People from Military Service
news.text:
The House rejected an amendment to the National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA) on Tuesday that would codify President Donald Trump’s executive order banning transgender-identifying people from military service.

Four Republicans joined Democrats to kill the amendment, sponsored by Rep. Lauren Boebert (R-CO), by a slim margin of 212-217. The four Republicans who voted with Democrats are Reps. Brian Fitzpatrick (R-PA) and Mike Lawler (R-NY) — who both represents districts that voted for Kamala Harris — and Reps. James Moylan from Guam and Kimberlyn King-Hinds from the Northern Mariana Islands.

“Four ‘republicans’ just betrayed President Trump and our military in favor of the Transgenders,” Boebert said in a post to X. “DISGUSTING!”

The amendment would have codified President Trump’s January 2025 executive order stating that “consistent with the military mission and longstanding DoD policy, expressing a false ‘gender identity’ divergent from an individual’s sex cannot satisfy the rigorous standards necessary for military service.” 

“Beyond the hormonal and surgical medical interventions involved, adoption of a gender identity inconsistent with an individual’s sex conflicts with a soldier’s commitment to an honorable, truthful, and disciplined lifestyle, even in one’s personal life,” the order reads. “A man’s assertion that he is a woman, and his requirement that others honor this falsehood, is not consistent with the humility and selflessness required of a service member.”

The president’s order states that the armed forces “shall neither allow males to use or share sleeping, changing, or bathing facilities designated for females, nor allow females to use or share sleeping, changing, or bathing facilities designated for males.” 

In June, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia voted 2-1 against Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s decision to expel transgender-identifying people. The ruling blocked Hegseth from expelling 28 transgender-identifying individuals who filed a lawsuit against the Trump administration over the order. The ruling did not block the administration’s rule barring new transgender enlistees. 

While Boebert’s amendment was blocked, two transgender-related amendments from Rep. Nancy Mace (R-SC) passed the House.

One of her amendments, which would ban the military health insurance plan, called TRICARE, from covering sex change drugs and surgeries, passed 219-208, with five Democrats siding with Republicans. Her other amendment, which would ban transgender-identifying males from playing on girls’ K-12 sports teams at Department of Defense-run schools, passed 221-203.

“Both of my NDAA amendments targeting mentally ill transgenders — and protecting our women and girls — passed the floor of the U.S. House of Representatives tonight with flying colors,” Mace said in a post to X on Tuesday. 

The NDAA is an annual bill passed to establish policies and appropriations for the Department of Defense and national security programs in the Department of Energy. 

Katherine Hamilton is a political reporter for Breitbart News. You can follow her on X @thekat_hamilton.
news.summary:
The House rejected an amendment to the NDAA that would codify Trump's executive order banning trans-identifying people from military service.

news.author: Katherine Hamilton

news.title: Rosie O'Donnell Claims She Has 'Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder' After 20-Year-Old Feud With 'Idiot' Trump: 'He Used Me as a Punchline'
news.text:
Rosie O’Donnell has changed her attitude over the years, and RadarOnline.com can reveal that her 20-year-old feud with President Trump has heavily impacted her life.

The 64-year-old looked back at her battle with Trump and declared that now that she's older, she doesn't care what is said about her.'Been 20 Years of Him Using Me as a Punchline'"It used to be when our president would say things about me, it would be like, whoa," O'Donnell told People. "First of all, if you had written this as a movie, a stand-up comic makes fun of a reality show host who is a bloviating idiot and he ends up being the president, and then he doesn't shut up about her for 20 years, it would be unbelievable – and the fact that it’s my life is very trippy."

After Trump beat Kamala Harris in the 2024 election and snagged a second term in the White House, O'Donnell left the country and moved to Ireland. And according to her 13-year-old son, Clay, the comic isn't as cutting-edge since changing residences. 

“I am softer, I think, as a result of being there, of the welcome that we got, and as a result of me not being constantly bombarded by news about the current president," O’Donnell noted in her interview.

The Harriet the Spy star then compared her situation to "post-traumatic stress disorder for me," and added, "Considering it's been 20 years of him using me as a punchline."Rosie O'Donnell Takes Over for Trump Rival Jimmy Kimmel"So I think that has allowed me to reclaim myself, who I was before all that went down, even though it's very strange to have supposedly the most powerful man in the world be your arch nemesis," O'Donnell added.

O'Donnell, who credited Clay's "vulnerability" to help her be "more connected," is currently in New York City for the summer, where she will be guest hosting Jimmy Kimmel Live!, while another one of Trump's rivals, Jimmy Kimmel, is on vacation.

"I'm very happy that (Kimmel) asked me,” O'Donnell said. "I admire him. I admire his tenacity and strength. I admire his conviction to speak out against this political terrorism that's happening in our country, and I stand beside him and will..."

Kimmel, like O'Donnell in the past, is no stranger to squaring off against the controversial president, and his repeated insults have led to Trump repeatedly trying to have him pulled from the air.Trump Mocks Rosie O'Donnell AgainMeanwhile, while O'Donnell has seemed to cool off on bashing Trump, the same can't be said for the divisive politician. Earlier this month, the 80-year-old posted an AI video of himself as a doctor diagnosing celebrities with "Trump Derangement Syndrome," or TDS.

One of the celebrities in the baffling video? An AI-generated version of O'Donnell. "I have been suffering for over a decade, and after listening to Dr. Trump, I can see some results," fake O'Donnell says in the clip.

O'Donnell was joined by other celebrities in the AI clip, including The Odyssey actor John Leguizamo, O'Donnell's former The View co-host, Whoopi Goldberg, and movie stars Ed Norton, Robert De Niro, and Julia Roberts.

"Turn off fake news. Say your prayers, and if you ever feel anxious, just have a Diet Coke like me, and you're gonna see a remarkable difference in your life," fake Trump advises in the video.Could Rosie O'Donnell Sue Trump?The bizarre video led to plenty of backlash, with one person raging at the time, "Embarrassing that our President is resorting to these sorts of carnival videos, but he is, as always, a carnival barker conman. 

"Can you imagine this being posted by a previous President or a respected world leader?" another asked. 

According to entertainment and media lawyer Paul Menes, the celebrities in the video could sue. He explained, "Utilizing their names, images, and likenesses without their consent violates their right of publicity in California and several other states' laws."
news.summary: Rosie O'Donnell is looking back at her lengthy feud with Donald Trump, revealing she feels like she has 'PTSD.'
news.author: Bryan Brunati

news.title: GOP Has Twice the Cash on Hand for Midterms as Democrats. It's Actually Worse Than That.
news.text:
I did a double take when I saw the fundraising totals for the midterm elections in Politico. Republican party committees and super PACs have raised $657 million for the midterms, now just four and a half months away. That's nearly double the $337 million the Democrats have raised.

And it gets worse for the Democrats. The largest single financial entity raising money for the midterms, MAGA, Inc., reports $400 million in cash as of June 30. 

That billion-dollar-plus war chest is three times as much as the Democrats' cash entering the final months of the campaign.

Money isn't quite the dominant force it used to be in politics. Kamala Harris raised more than $1.2 billion in 2024 and lost to Trump's $800 million. But if you ask a candidate if they would rather have more money than their opponent, the answer would be "yes." Trump made up that shortfall by tapping super PACs that supported him. But the PACs cannot coordinate with the campaign over targeting and dividing resources. This is a disadvantage, but not a large one. Trump obviously managed just fine. 

Still, what the massive discrepancy shows is dysfunction in the Democrats' administrative departments. And it has the potential to cost them if Republicans are smart about where they put all that cash.

“What is putting the House majority and the Senate majority, nationally, in danger is the Republicans’ massive stockpile of resources that they’re putting together to push back,” said Morgan Jackson, a longtime North Carolina Democratic strategist and an adviser to former Gov. Roy Cooper, who is running for Senate.“If you get outspent five to six to eight to one, that can alleviate the environmental advantage that Democrats have this cycle of poor leadership from the White House, a dysfunctional Congress, a bad economy that they own,” he added. “There’s a lot of concern to that.”Republicans have a massive cash advantage among official party committees: The Republican National Committee has $128 million in the bank compared to just $16 million for the Democratic National Committee, which is actually in the red because it has $18 million in debt. And in the fight for control of Congress, both the National Republican Senatorial Committee and National Republican Congressional Committee have larger war chests than their Democratic counterparts — the NRSC has $55.9 million to the DSCC’s $41 million and the NRCC has $92.7 million to the DCCC’s $79 million.

A June 30 Supreme Court decision allowed closer cooperation between party committees and campaigns. Under the Federal Election Campaign Act (FECA), party committees were permitted to work side-by-side with candidates on strategy, advertising, and messaging, but their coordinated spending was subject to specific dollar limits termed "Coordinated Party Expenditures." The Supreme Court struck down these spending limits, allowing political party committees to spend unlimited amounts in direct coordination with candidates.

The Republican National Committee has $129 million in cash on hand. The Democrats are still $2 million in debt. That bespeaks an organization that's floundering, unable to raise money to pay what it owes. 

The outlook is dismal, but Democrats are pretending "all is well."

The super PAC disparity is different than in previous cycles: At this time in 2024, SMP had slightly more cash on hand than SLF, while CLF’s advantage over HMP was only $22 million. But Republican fundraising has taken off since Trump’s return to the White House, while Democratic super PAC funding has held more steady.Democratic super PACs dismiss the Republican cash advantage. Lauren French, spokesperson for SMP, said the GOP’s fundraising reflected “pay-to-play” with large donors rather than momentum.“Republicans can crow all they want about getting massive checks from donors and businesses and billionaires, but we’re going to win because we have the actual support from people who are voting,” she said.

Oh, I forgot. The Democrats are the party of the "little guy." They have plenty of their own billionaires and major corporations supporting them. That "little guy" bit may have worked with Obama but modern Democrats are just as besotted with big-money contributions as the GOP.

It's a different story at the candidate level where the enthusiasm of the far left is most keenly felt. "In Georgia, Sen. Jon Ossoff has $40 million more in the bank than his GOP challenger, Rep. Mike Collins, according to recent FEC filings," according to Politico. "In Texas, James Talarico has nearly $20 million more than Attorney General Ken Paxton, and in North Carolina, Cooper has $17 million more than former RNC Chair Michael Whatley."

“Too many people think that fragmentation of media meant more efficiency and spending less. Wrong — it means you have to spend more,” said Jesse Ferguson, a longtime Democratic strategist. With so many media options, “you have to be everywhere they are to get the reach and frequency to make a point," he says.

So maybe the Democrats' paucity of resources may make the difference in November anyway.
