news.title: Shapiro-Ossoff would be the Dems’ best 2028 hope, except …
news.text:
Shapiro currently boasts a 56% approval rating in a state Trump won twice and narrowly lost once. Down south, Ossoff has a 5.5% lead over Republican Mike Collins in the RCP average of that Senate race in a state also won by Trump twice and narrowly lost once. 

Winning key swing states is the ballgame in presidential elections. And having a Shapiro-Ossoff ticket, or Ossoff-Shapiro ticket, would certainly help capture two very big prizes in gaining 270 electoral votes.

But the problem, of course, is that both men are Jewish. And in a world where more and more Democrats are embracing anti-Israel, antisemitic candidates, it’s hard to see how Shapiro and Ossoff can emerge from the primaries, where the far-left has all the money, momentum, and organization. 

Today’s Democratic platform is to turn Israel, our staunchest ally, into the enemy. The most recent and despicable example of this new war on Israel from the Left comes via a House vote to strip the country of all offensive and defensive funding from the United States. The defensive part is particularly egregious since that would include the Iron Dome, a highly effective missile defense system that has saved countless civilian lives and perhaps prevented Israel from being wiped off the map entirely. 

“Israel is a first-world country, and it can pay for the defensive systems it needs,” Rep. Ro Khanna (D-CA) argued while repeatedly accusing Israel of committing “genocide.” “We should not be subsidizing them, especially given their egregious violations of human rights law.”

Overall, more than 100 Democrats joined Khanna, a 2028 presidential hopeful, in voting against funding the Iron Dome. Khanna is the same lawmaker who staunchly supported Graham Platner, whom Khanna stood by until a rape allegation by an ex-girlfriend made it politically impossible to do so.

Khanna is also the same guy who has appeared on Hasan Piker’s show, another profound antisemitic radical socialist who has said that he believes Hamas is “1,000 times better” than Israel and that the U.S. “deserved 9/11.”

“I would go [on Piker’s] show” again, proclaimed Khanna, despite this kind of antisemitism, with the California lawmaker arguing that it’s important to try to reach new kinds of voters, including those who are fans of Piker for one big obvious reason. 

Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-MA) also backed Platner and, on Monday, released a video on X enthusiastically endorsing Abdul el Sayed in Michigan, who recently campaigned with Piker. And just last month, a former staffer of el Sayed was indicted for allegedly conspiring to “terrorize” leaders at the University of Michigan in Ann Arbor into severing all ties with Israel. 

El Sayed has called Bibi Netanyahu a war criminal and has also called for defunding the police. 

But no matter: It’s all about regaining the Senate, even if it means sacrificing all integrity, which Warren did long ago.

Van Jones, formerly of the Obama administration and currently of CNN, has had enough of the antisemitic rise in his party. 

“Supporting Hamas is not progressive,” he wrote on social media this week in a post that went viral. “The idea of ‘no police, no prisons, no border’” — which would let pedophiles, human traffickers, terrorists, hate groups and crime syndicates run wild — is not progressive for vulnerable communities. Celebrating the murder of Israeli civilians is not progressive. These ideas are regressive.

“If we can’t tell right from wrong, good from bad, smart from loony tunes — Team Blue will go down the same road that turned the Republican Party into a shell of itself,” he wrote. “We can’t let that happen to the Democratic Party.”

Jones, along with Sen. John Fetterman (D-PA), is one of the few examples of establishment Democrats pushing back against the loony tunes Jones described. 

“If our party ever becomes, and just make it official, as the anti-Israel party … that’s when I would leave because that’s a bit of moral clarity for me,” Fetterman declared recently. 

Add it all up, and Shapiro and/or Ossoff are going to have major difficulties in Democratic primaries. Kamala Harris, the 2024 installed nominee after then-President Joe Biden dropped out, provided a preview of this challenge during her presidential campaign two years ago when tasked with choosing a running mate. The smart money was on Shapiro, a popular governor in a must-win state, but he was strangely passed over for goofy Gov. Tim Walz of Minnesota.

Shapiro later shared in his book, Where We Keep The Light, that during his vetting process, he was asked if he’d been in contact with undercover Israeli agents at the time. 

“I wondered whether these questions were being posed to just me — the only Jewish guy in the running — or if everyone who had not held a federal office was being grilled about Israel in the same way,” Shapiro wrote. 

“What’s even the point of asking that question in that way?” asked Aaron Keyak, a board member of the Combat Antisemitism Movement, in an interview with ABC News. “Were they trying to send some sort of message to Gov. Shapiro? Were they trying to intimidate him?” 

Keyak also once served on a task force at the State Department to monitor and combat antisemitism. 

Ossoff, 39, will obviously face the same high hurdles if he defeats Republican Mike Collins in his Senate race in November. According to a new AP-NORC poll, 58% of Democrats say the U.S. is “too supportive” of Israel. This is a decisive shift from just two years ago, when just 45% of Democrats felt the same way in the same poll, marking a 13-point shift. 

Shapiro-Ossoff. Ossoff-Shapiro. 

A majority of the people they represent in key swing states believe they’re doing a good job. The national conversation around their possible presidential possibilities is growing as a result. 

But there’s an insidious underbelly to the conversation. 

Could each win against JD Vance and/or Marco Rubio in 2028? Perhaps. 

Will either or both get the opportunity? Probably not. 

This is a party veering off in a very dangerous and ugly direction. A direction that could cost them elections in 2028 and the years to come.

news.title: Jimmy Kimmel thanks CBS for record-breaking June ratings after network axed Stephen Colbert’s show
news.text:
Jimmy Kimmel has sent his thanks to CBS for inadvertently boosting the ratings for his eponymous late night show, after the network axed The Late Show With Stephen Colbert.

Colbert’s final broadcast aired in mid-May, forcing his dedicated viewers to tune into another late night show. Now, it seems that many have chosen Kimmel’s.In June, the show averaged 3.15 million viewers for its best-ever monthly audience in its 23-year history.

“The ratings are very strong, which we are very grateful to CBS for their wisdom,” Kimmel slyly told Variety. “We were doing pretty well before that, but that really helped.”

Asked if he thought Colbert fans had found a new home, he quipped: “I’ve always thought of our show as an orphanage.”

CBS announced the cancellation of the long-running Late Show franchise last July, just days after Colbert criticized the network’s parent company, Paramount, for reaching a $16 million settlement with President Donald Trump over accusations that its series 60 Minutes deceptively edited a 2024 interview with then-Vice President Kamala Harris.

The final episode of The Late Show with Stephen Colbert attracted an audience of 6.74 million viewers, making it the most-watched weeknight episode during the show’s 11-year run.

More follows on this breaking news story. Subscribe here to get the latest updates from The Independent
news.summary: In June, ‘Jimmy Kimmel Live!’ broke a ratings record across its 23-year history

news.title: Democrats Dismiss Our Constitutional Traditions as 'Nostalgia'
news.text:
It appears that the Madisonian democracy has joined shackets and combat boots as embarrassingly outdated for many on the left. In calling for radical changes to our constitutional system, leading Democrats are now calling the lingering loyalty to our traditions as mere “nostalgia.” To be nostalgic in today’s parlance is to be a dupe of the oligarchs and an enemy to reform.

“Nostalgia” has become the new coded term for reactionaries among Democratic figures, who are trying to convince Americans to accept radical changes to our republic after 250 years.

Kamala Harris recently insisted that opposition to ideas like packing the Supreme Court is mere “nostalgia” for a system that is no longer working. “I would caution us against talking about rebuilding with any sense of nostalgia about how things work, because even before, they weren’t working so well for a lot of folks,” she said. That “nostalgia,” according to Harris, is preventing us from doing things like packing the Supreme Court with an instant liberal majority.

California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) last week also declared that “nostalgia is not working” and, while refusing to embrace socialism, added that “capitalism as we know it doesn’t work.”

news.title: Huge blow for Gavin Newsom as shock polls show Democrat 2028 White House rival streaking ahead
news.text:
Gov. Gavin Newsom is poised to make a run as a front-running candidate in the Democratic primary for the White House in 2028 — but new polling suggests another California Democrat is the more popular choice to be US president.

Former Vice President Kamala Harris has a significant advantage over Newsom and the rest of the field by comfortable margins, in the most recent 14 major polls, suggesting she has the early advantage in a still-fluid race.

A poll by Echelon Insights from July 9 to 13 shows Harris with a 7 percentage point lead ahead of Newsom. A Quantus Insights poll from July 3 to 7 has Harris 18 points ahead, with 35% of the vote share.

Harris’ streak of being in the top spot stretches all the way back to late May.

The last poll that didn’t show Harris leading was an Emerson College survey conducted May 24–25, which put former Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg ahead with a 2-point lead over Newsom, who drew 16%, while Harris trailed at 10%.

Since then, Newsom has consistently ranked second behind Harris, polling between 15% and 20%. While early presidential polls often reflect name recognition more than eventual outcomes, they can shape perceptions of a candidate’s viability.

Harris has hinted at a White House repeat after losing the presidential election to Donald Trump in 2024. She has made moves that could signal shoring up support, such as reaching out to New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani, an important figure in the far-left base where she will need support.

Newsom has positioned himself as someone who is repeatedly on the offensive against President Donald Trump.

Some of Newsom’s recent actions — such as his embrace of a national billionaire tax while simultaneously opposing a statewide one — could be seen as an attempt by Newsom to retain attraction from the far-left base and cut into Harris’ lead.

“This whole jiu-jitsu he has to do to oppose the [billionaire] tax in his state and then support it federally, it doesn’t sound as authentic as others might sound on the soapbox at the Iowa State Fair,” GOP strategist Rob Stutzman told CalMatters.

Download The California Post App, follow us on social, and subscribe to our newsletters
news.summary: The two Californians are expected to be serious contenders for the White House in 2028.

news.title: About Last Night: DSA Flops in AZ As Trump Ascends
news.text:
So much for the rising tide of Democratic Socialism. The DSA just discovered the limits of its reach in Arizona's 4th Congressional District, even as the Democrat Party struggles to contain its radical upstarts from splitting the party. Meanwhile, Donald Trump continues to demonstrate his strength in a relatively united GOP. He got a clean sweep for his slate of candidates in Arizona, and celebrated in an all-caps message this morning on Truth Social:

MAGA CANDIDATES WON EVERYTHING LAST NIGHT IN ARIZONA, A COMPLETE SWEEP!!! I WON ARIZONA 18 MONTHS AGO. THE DUMOCRATS ARE DUMBFOUNDED. AFTER DECADES, ARIZONA IS BECOMING A REPUBLICAN STATE. CONGRATULATIONS TO ALL! President DONALD J. TRUMP

That is premature at best. Democrats still hold most statewide elected offices, including both US Senate seats, neither of which is at stake in this midterm cycle. (Republican Treasurer Kimberly Yee is the sole exception.) Trump had a lot of success in the primaries, however, and seems to have won without creating major rifts in Arizona:

A full slate of Republican candidates endorsed by President Trump, including former NFL kicker Jay Feely, emerged victorious Tuesday in Arizona’s primary elections while a Democratic socialist House hopeful suffered a crushing defeat. Feely, who played for six teams — including the Jets and Giants — during his 14 seasons as a pro, defeated former state lawmaker Joseph Chaplik in the Grand Canyon State’s 1st Congressional District, which covers northeastern Maricopa County and is the wealthiest in Arizona.The 1st District seat is being vacated by Rep. David Schweikert (R-Ariz.), who opted to run for governor. Democrats have targeted the district as a key flip opportunity as they try to regain the House majority after four years out of power.

That's a reach for Democrats. Arizona's 1st CD has a Cook index of R+1, but Trump won it by three points in the last election, and Yee won it by nearly seven points two years before that. However, Democrats won it more often than not in the past two decades. Feely's easy win last night contrasts with the Democrats' too-close-to-call result in AZ-01, where prior losing candidate Amish Shah has a narrow lead over two other candidates at the moment. Shah had earlier declared that he was running even more to the Left after his 2024 loss, which seems out of step for a slightly Republican district on the nicer outskirts of the Phoenix area:

During a Congressional District 1 Zoom candidate forum hosted by the Legislative District 3 Democrats on April 29, Shah, a four-term Democrat in the state House, reflected on his 2024 loss and told attendees that he plans to do things a little differently this election cycle.Shah attributed his loss to three factors: underperformance at the top of the ticket with former Vice President Kamala Harris in her presidential race; the late Arizona primary election date giving candidates a shorter amount of time to campaign for the general election; and groups such as the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee telling him he was too nice during his campaign — leading Shah to accept a challenge to use expletives to “prosecute (President Donald Trump and Republicans) as vigorously” as he could.

The NRCC responded with a bring it on at the time:

“Democrat Amish Shah said he lost because Biden and Harris were toxic, then ran even further left by calling for massive tax increases, socialism and open borders. Amish Shah’s radical statements will haunt him long after this race to the far-left primary,” NRCC Spokesperson Ben Petersen told the Yellow Sheet Report.As evidence of Shah’s support for “tax increases, socialism and open borders,” the NRCC pointed to Shah’s opposition to Trump’s One Big Beautiful Bill Act, H.R. 1, his support for universal healthcare and lowering premiums, and calling Trump’s border and immigration policies “lawless.”

That's not the really bad news for progressives from last night's primaries, however. That took place in AZ-04, where the DSA attempted to push out incumbent Greg Stanton with upstart Kai Newkirk. Until now, the DSA has succeeded in deep-blue congressional districts by catching incumbent Democrats asleep at the switch. This time, however, the DSA attempted it in a district with a D+4 Cook index, and got clobbered as a result:

In the 4th District, centered in the Phoenix suburbs of Tempe and Mesa, incumbent Rep. Greg Stanton (D-Ariz.) swamped Democratic socialist primary challenger Kai Newkirk by 30 percentage points.Newkirk, who accused Stanton of being too supportive of Israel and corporations and slammed the congressman’s vote in favor of the Laken Riley Act mandating illegal immigrants who are arrested, charged or convicted of “burglary, theft, larceny or shoplifting” be held by immigration authorities.

Well, whaddaya know. Turns out that immigration enforcement and not siding with terrorists against Jews pays off in straight-up election fights, at least in most of America. That should give Democrats a ringing lesson on the need to marginalize the DSA and its activists in future election cycles, although their situation may be too far gone to do much in the upcoming general election. Stanton will face conservative activist Zuhdi Jasser in November; Jasser ran unopposed in the primary on a platform featuring pledges for border enforcement, budget control, and strong national security. That's going to be an uphill battle for Jasser, but at least the DSA won't get to poach another House seat.

This lesson may prove prophetic for Democrats in other states, where the DSA wants to run where the electorates are far less radical. Michigan's Democrats may deny Abdul El-Sayed a Senate nomination, but Wisconsin Democrats look ready to nominate Francesca Hong in Wisconsin:

Democratic socialists predict another victory for their movement may arrive in an unlikely place: battleground Wisconsin, where state Rep. Francesca Hong, a democratic socialist running for governor, is testing the state's purple waters."Regardless of generation, but especially for young people, they are demanding a different type of politics and a different type of politician," Hong said in an interview with ABC News. ...While reliable public polling has been scant, a statewide poll from Marquette in March found Hong pulling a slight lead over top contenders in the Aug. 11 Democratic primary, including former Lt. Gov. Mandela Barnes and current Lt. Gov. Sara Rodriguez, who dropped out of the race on Friday over a campaign finance controversy. But in another shakeup of the race, Gov. Tony Evers on Saturday endorsed David Crowley, the Milwaukee County executive. Crowley officially relaunched his campaign for governor after suspending it just days earlier and endorsing Rodriguez.

Wisconsin Democrats are a mess at the moment, but they're not giving Hong a chance at a default win that would make her into a punching bag for Republicans. The DSA can't compete outside of radicalized localities, as Democrats discovered last night, and may learn the harder way in November if they can't stop El-Sayed and Hong in the next three weeks.

news.title: Some Trump Voters Think Gas Would Have Been Cheaper if Kamala Harris Won
news.text: Some voters who backed President Donald Trump in the 2024 election believe gas would have been cheaper if former Vice President Kamala Harris, a Democrat, had won, according to a new poll ahead of the 2026 midterm elections, when the economy will be a key focus. According to the Politico poll released on Wednesday, voters who cast their ballots for Trump in the most recent election said gas prices “have gone up more under Donald Trump than they would have under a Democratic president.” Thirty-five percent said gas prices would have gone up the same amount under a Democrat, while only 34 percent of Trump’s 2024 voters believe gas prices would have gone up more under a Democratic administration. The Iran war has put pressure on the global oil supply, resulting in higher prices at the pump across the globe. Disruptions to the Strait of Hormuz, a critical passageway for oil, caused prices to soar since the United States and Israel launched strikes against Iran in February. Although prices fell during the ceasefire of the conflict, they have begun to rise again amid renewed fighting in recent weeks. High gas prices carry political risk for Trump, who made tackling inflation a cornerstone of his 2024 campaign and attacked former President Joe Biden, a Democrat, over high prices in the aftermath of the COVID-19 pandemic. Frustrations over the economy were viewed as a major force that propelled Trump back to the Oval Office. Nearly two years later, polls suggest Americans are unhappy with the state of the economy and disapprove of the president’s handling of it. Democrats have made opposition to the war, as well as highlighting its impact on gas prices, central to their message for the midterms, when they are hoping to retake control of the House of Representatives and the Senate. Newsweek reached out to the White House for comment via email. Americans Blame High Gas Prices on Iran War Overall, a plurality of 42 percent of respondents said gas prices have gone up more than they would have if Harris won, while only 18 percent said they believed gas prices would have gone up more under a Democratic administration. The poll also asked respondents about the reasons they believe gas prices have risen. Most Americans—63 percent—said the Iran war is a factor in why gas prices have risen. Forty percent said Trump’s tariffs have played a role, while 38 percent said his “poor handling of the economy” was to blame. Twenty-three percent pointed to supply chain disruptions while 21 percent blamed high gas prices on a lack of domestic oil production. The online poll, conducted by Politico and Public First, surveyed 2,061 adults from July 12-15, 2026. White House spokesperson Kush Desai has previously said Trump has “always been clear about short-term disruptions as a result of Operation Epic Fury, disruptions that the Administration has been diligently working to mitigate.” The Iran war began on February 28, paused during a brief ceasefire starting around April 7-8 before hostilities resumed earlier in July. Americans Frustrated With Gas Prices, Economy Amid Iran War: Polls Other recent polls also show that Americans are unhappy with the state of the economy. A poll from The Washington Post and Ipsos found that only 33 percent of Americans approve of Trump’s handling of the economy—the lowest point in any surveys from the pollster. Only 39 percent of respondents said they were confident negotiations to end the war would bring gas prices down, while 59 percent said they were not confident in that. The poll surveyed 2,648 adults from July 8-13, 2026 and had a margin of error of plus or minus 1.9 percentage points. A CBS News/YouGov poll measuring Americans’ attitudes toward the Iran war found that 23 percent viewed gas prices as a “financial hardship” while an additional 30 percent said it has been “difficult.” Twenty-nine percent described gas prices as an “inconvenience,” while only 7 percent said their finances had not been impacted by the rising gas prices. It found that 7 percent of Americans view the economy as “very good,” while 24 percent say it is “fairly good.” Thirty-three percent said it is “fairly bad,” while 30 percent described the condition of the economy as “very bad.” It surveyed 2,519 adults from June 17-19, 2026 and had a margin of error of plus or minus 2.4 percentage points. Trump told reporters on Wednesday that Americans “don’t want high gasoline prices, but they’re not against the war.” The Ipsos poll found that 68 percent of Americans do not believe the war is worth fighting, while YouGov/CBS News found that 78 percent of Americans want the war to end now. How Much Does a Gallon of Gas Cost? Nationwide, gas prices averaged $4.060 per gallon on Wednesday, up from $4.019 on Tuesday and $3.890 one week earlier, according to AAA. Gas cost $3.929 per gallon one month earlier and $3.145 one year earlier. That marks a 29 percent increase in gas prices from one year ago. California saw the highest prices with an average of $5.545 per gallon on Wednesday, according to AAA. Mississippi saw the lowest prices at $3.622 per gallon. GasBuddy analyst Patrick De Haan wrote in an X post Wednesday that gas prices could continue rising in the coming weeks. “Crude oil prices continue to advance as [Houthis] threaten Saudi oil route through Bab al-Mandab Strait, WTI up >3% to $87, Brent up 3.5% to $94, RBOB up 7c/gal. based on where things stand now, national average could rise to $4.15-$4.25/gal in the next couple weeks,” he wrote. How the Iran War Is Driving Up Gas Prices The Iran war has been a major strain on the global oil supply, with disruptions to the Strait of Hormuz leading to a spike in prices. The strait lies between Iran to the north and Oman to the south, with shipping lanes passing through the territorial waters of both countries, but the strait is governed by international maritime law. Iran exerts significant influence because of its long northern coastline and control of nearby islands that overlook the narrow strait, while Oman oversees the southern side. The waterway is also a critical route for liquefied natural gas (LNG), carrying about 20 percent of global LNG trade, largely from Qatar. Most of the oil shipped through the strait is bound for Asian markets, making disruptions especially significant for global energy prices. Contact Newsweek editors on this story: Jenni Fink and Sam Wilson.
news.summary: Gas prices have again surpassed $4 per gallon nationally amid renewed fighting in the Iran war, according to AAA data.
news.author: Andrew Stanton

news.title: Actually, no. Kamala Harris doesn't have a shot in 2028.
news.text:
There's an idea taking hold on the left that former Vice President Kamala Harris is a viable choice for the Democratic presidential nomination in 2028. She isn't. And even if she somehow won it, she'd be a certain loser in the general election.

A recent New York magazine piece by David Freedlander was headlined "Like It or Not, Kamala Harris Is the Front-runner." Her advisers now treat it as an article of faith that the compressed campaign cost her the chance to define herself in 2024.

If anything, it did her a favor. The 107 days between President Joe Biden's exit and Election Day, the same "107 Days" she titled her memoir after, meant no real primary and less time having to defend an unpopular administration. She got to run as a blank slate instead of a scrutinized nominee. The thing her team calls a handicap was actually her cover.

She's leading early primary polls by a wide margin. One recent survey had her at 36%, more than triple her nearest rival. But that's mainly about name recognition. 

Prediction markets, where people put real money on the outcome instead of just answering a pollster's phone call, have her far lower.

Kamala Harris' problem, in one sentence

In Freedlander's conversations with Democratic insiders, the stale metaphors arrive early, which is never a good sign. "You don't have to be a rocket scientist to understand that right now we need leaders that can take us in a different direction," says Minyon Moore, a Democratic operative.

Then this, from an unnamed supporter convinced that Harris is running: "She is looking for where the wave is going and is determined to ride it."

That's Harris' whole problem in one sentence. Donald Trump didn't ride any waves. Neither did Barack Obama. They were the waves. Voters can tell the difference between someone who generates energy and someone searching for a current to ride.

The same supporter: "Her mindset is that I should have won, and I am going to do it my way. … It was all these consultants … it was their fault."

What was "her way," exactly? Nobody says. But it's clear that the border, the economy and taxpayer-funded gender surgery for inmates hurt Democrats badly – and Harris doubling down on all of it, even as the race slipped away, hurt her specifically. Her former staffers blame the money and the ground game instead. Of course they do.

"People are looking forward," says Laura Telerski, a New Hampshire state rep who's met Harris but hasn't endorsed her. "I don't think she's bogged down in yesterday." 

"Look how quickly voters forgot about Trump's first term," adds an unnamed Democratic official.

In other words, they're betting Americans forget how the Biden-Harris years actually went.

But voters didn't return to Trump because they forgot who he was. They went back because they remembered the economy was better and the border was under control. Harris' "fascist dictatorship" routine didn't square with people's memories of a Trump term that had, in fact, been just fine.

One anonymous former aide bragged that Harris "comes in with 99% name ID, and everyone else is going to have to raise money and buy it." 

True, but total name recognition just means there's no room left to grow, and the former vice president's favorability has sat underwater since the day she left office.

What Democratic voters actually want in 2028

If Trump's rise taught Democrats anything, it's that voters will forgive plenty of rough edges for the real thing, which is why candidates like Rep. Jasmine Crockett of Texas, Sen. John Fetterman of Pennsylvania and Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez of New York have drawn the party's energy in recent years. 

It's why Democrats are gravitating toward candidates who come across as raw and unfiltered rather than manufactured and stage-managed.

The problem with Harris isn't that she's too moderate or too far left. She can lurch left as easily as she's lurched everywhere else. Her problem is that she isn't anything in particular: not authentic, not much of a populist, certainly not working-class and not even a committed democratic socialist. She'll be whomever the base needs, and the quotes above prove it.

Losing nominees rarely get a second act. Harris might be the exception for a simple reason: She's still technically first in line in the Democratic Party until she says otherwise. As a Black Asian woman, she's even more protected. Democrats handed her the nomination in 2024 rather than be seen passing over the first minority woman vice president for another White man. The same thinking that made her impossible to skip in 2024 makes her nearly impossible to skip past now, unless she rules herself out first.

In the magazine story, Freedlander admits a contest against Harris "could get messy," because women and voters of color anchor the Democratic coalition, and being seen defending those groups matters to the party's identity as much as winning does.

When he asked Democratic donors and operatives what they make of a "Kamala 2.0," a couple were candid. "I hope she runs, because then she will lose and we will never have to hear from her again," says Democratic strategist Alex Hoffman. "She will never win an election at a national scale. Never."

But most gave Freedlander a particularly damning response: a heavy sigh, followed by a long pause. Everyone knows exactly what that pause means, but nobody can say it out loud.

Daniel Allott, USA TODAY's conservative opinion editor, is author of the book "On the Road in Trump's America: A Journey Into the Heart of a Divided Nation."

Reporting by Daniel Allott, USA TODAY / USA TODAY

news.title: INGERSOLL: The Thing That Never Happens Keeps Happening
news.text:
Below is State of the Day, a morning newsletter by Daily Caller Editor-at-Large Geoff Ingersoll. Sign up here.

Greetings, Dear Reader,

OOPS! Do you remember that thing we said never happens? 

It keeps happening.

THE THING THAT NEVER HAPPENS KEEPS HAPPENING

Sixteen hundred in Oregon. Fifteen hundred in California. There were more than 6,000 in Virginia. Some in Utah. Georgia and Michigan. Even Texas! A whopping 97,000 in Arizona marked as having shown “proof of citizenship” actually did no such thing. In fact, many if not most of those did the exact opposite. They marked “non-citizen” and were nevertheless still registered to vote.

The burgeoning voter roll scandal in New Jersey is very similar to an MO that’s now become standard: This isn’t happening. It’s “rare” and “isolated.” Oops, the thing that never happens actually happened quite a bit.

But don’t worry, it’s just a software bug! It’s just a few thousand!

Newly sworn in NJ Gov. Mikie Sherrill said that 6,600 non-citizens were registered to vote in New Jersey despite ticking “non-citizen” at the DMV because of a “software error,” and that she intended to “replace” the “vendor responsible.”

Just one small problem for Sherrill: The “vendor responsible” says Sherrill’s own Elections Commission is the responsible party.

It’s a real whodunnit, eh?

Not really. It’s the process that’s the problem.

Way back in the beforetimes of 2019 when people were probably a bit more honest than they would have been had they gotten a peek at the future, Kamala Harris said, “The best and most secure way to conduct elections? Paper ballots … Russia can’t hack a piece of paper.”

Paper ballots indeed. Of course she wasn’t referring to in-person voting, but at the time, many of these machines were doing “electronic voting.” Kind of like going to an ATM kiosk.

But it turns out you pretty much can hack a piece of paper. In hacker circles, they’d call the way to do it a “man in the middle” attack.

Gemini defines MIM as “a cybersecurity threat where an attacker secretly intercepts, relays, or alters communications between two parties who believe they are communicating directly with each other.”

So if Javier Vasquez is trying to be honest as he applies for a non-citizen driving license, all it takes is sticking the right people between Vasquez’s submitted form and the end point of voter registration and bango, you’ve got one more guy on the roll.

If you think about it just a little bit harder, you’ll understand that MIM is exactly why Republicans hate voting processes that put bureaucrats and activists between the voter, his or her registration and the ballot box.

While wrong on the details, Harris was inadvertently correct in the broader sense. Analogue means of voting are tremendously more difficult to hack. If 99% of voters show up in person, cast a single hard copy vote, and the ballot box has airtight chain-of-custody, there’s almost no way to steal an election.

(Compromising registration, chain-of-custody, and stuffing boxes, as Kennedy’s allies did to Nixon in Illinois, is still possible, but the barrier to entry is gigantic.)

The wiggle room on the sickly and elderly and deployed active duty servicemen would be a tight squeeze for anyone planning a MIM attack on absentees. Almost certain they’d get caught if 95% of enlisted personnel up and voted for one candidate, for example.

No, it’s not the vendor or even the state. It’s the process that’s the problem for Republicans and the advantage for Democrats.

There’s so much noise and so many moving parts to election systems that, in some states, discourage identification and traditional registration, and sources votes (or “ballots”) in streams that put several people between the voter and the box.

It’s why, in part I’m sure, Michigan Sen. (and career spook) Elissa Slotkin flat out admitted that going back to widespread analogue voting – in person, with ID, and a paper ballot – would “rig our democracy” to make it “hard for any Democrat in any state to win any election.”

The way Democrats want to run elections is the way ABC runs “American Idol.” Remember, you can vote ten times on Facebook and ten times over text! 

The means of voting are so divorced from the civic act we all envision, how can you trust any of it? You really can’t. 

But that’s not a “software error.” It’s a feature. One that Dems will increasingly rely on to win future elections.

MORE LINKS:

Some political news for you this morning.

We need to be paying more attention to things like this.

Spineless leaders.
news.summary: It’s a real whodunnit, eh?
news.author: Daily Caller Editor,Geoffrey Ingersoll

news.title: Remorseless ex-NFL star Le'Veon Bell breaks his silence on child support arrest... as he reposts mugshot and shares wild three-word statement
news.text:
Former NFL running back Le'Veon Bell has broken his silence on his arrest for allegedly failing to pay a reported $50,000 in child support.

Bell was in Ohio on Sunday for a public event in Canton when he was arrested by local police on a warrant out of Michigan. On Tuesday, the 34-year-old former Pittsburgh Steelers star took to X to write: 'F*** CHILD SUPPORT.'

He included a crying emoji in the post.

It's unclear if Bell has been extradited to Michigan yet. The Daily Mail has sought comment from Bell through his attorney, Thomas Shaffer.

Shaffer previously said that the issue stems from Bell's child support payments being based on his NFL salary, which he hasn't earned since 2021, when he finished his career with the Tampa Bay Buccaneers.

'Six months ago he cashed in an NFL pension to pay $50,000 in child support for that child,' Shaffer told the Canton Repository.

'His guidelines are still inaccurately being deemed based on his NFL salary and his professional boxing career,' he said. 'This is a revolving door because he pays a number that doesn't reflect his true income. The issue will never end. The court needs to reflect his true income.'

Bell earned more than $45 million in salary over eight NFL seasons, per Spotrac.

The child in question reportedly lives in Michigan, where Bell once played for the Michigan State Spartans prior to his NFL career.

Bell has proven a divisive character since stepping away from the NFL in 2022, with his outlandish behavior on social media often sparking contention.

Amid his support for Donald Trump in the 2024 presidential election, the two-time First Team All-Pro drew heavy criticism after sharing a photo of himself in a T-shirt which labeled his rival Kamala Harris a 'tramp'.

A number of fans accused him of sexism and misogyny after the offensive slur about Harris, before he went on to taunt them after Trump cruised to victory in the election.

Just months later, Bell was forced to deny allegations that he raped his cousin for around a decade - starting when she was just a child.

That was despite a jury ordering in March off 2025 that the woman received damages totaling $25million from Bell and another $11.05m from his brother La'Vonte, according to court documents.

Both La'Vonte and Le'Veon were accused of starting nonconsensual 'incestual relations' with her when she was just six to seven years old. The civil complaint says they allegedly continued 'through at least 2017' - four years after Bell was drafted into the NFL.

An attorney for Bell initially told TMZ that he 'adamantly denies' the allegations in the case. Following the initial report, the ex-football player did a livestream on Twitch, calling the allegations 'bulls***' before boasting about his number of sexual partners.

'Do y'all think Le'Veon Bell... do I need to force myself upon a woman? Anybody? Do I need to force myself upon them? I don't,' he said.

'My number is so high... thousands of women, slayed by the dragon,' he later continued. 'I done slayed so many women it's like a f***ing dragon bro. I'm a dragon slayer out here.'

Bell denied ever forcing himself upon a woman sexually.

A second-round draft pick by the Steelers out of Michigan State in 2013, Bell played five ​seasons with the ​Steelers before ⁠sitting out the 2018 season in a contract dispute.

He continued his career with the ​New York Jets (2019-20), Kansas City Chiefs (2020), Baltimore Ravens (2021) ​and ⁠Tampa Bay Buccaneers (2021).

Bell made three Pro Bowls, earned two All-Pro first-team selections and produced three 1,000-yard seasons with the Steelers.

He ⁠retired ​with 9,843 yards from scrimmage (6,554 rushing ​and 3,289 receiving) and 51 touchdowns in 96 career games (81 starts).

After football, Bell ​went 2-1 as a professional boxer from 2022-24.
news.summary: Former NFL running back and failed boxer Le'Veon Bell has broken his silence on his arrest for allegedly failing to pay a reported $50,000 in child support payments.
news.author: Alex Raskin,Editor

news.title: 'A Noose Around His Neck': Author Michael Wolff Predicts Brutal Midterm Disaster for Donald Trump
news.text: Donald Trump is staring at a 'brutal' midterm reckoning in Washington, where author Michael Wolff predicts Congress will soon feel like 'a noose around his neck' after November's vote.Speaking about the looming elections in the United States, Wolff said Trump is so worried about losing control of Congress that he has already leaned into voter suppression measures and delivered a speech on Thursday defending tighter restrictions.The warning centres on what happens if Republicans are defeated in those midterm elections and Trump is left governing with a hostile legislature.Wolff, a long‑time Trump chronicler, argued on his podcast that the real humiliation for the former president will come not from Democrats alone, but from Republicans quietly peeling away.Michael Wolff's Stark Warning on the Donald Trump MidtermsMichael Wolff used his podcast Inside Trump's Head to sketch a bleak post‑midterm landscape for Donald Trump.In conversation with co‑host Joanna Coles, he argued that a newly empowered Congress, led by Democrats in the House, would become an almost suffocating constraint on the presidency.'Congress will become an incredible noose around his neck, a burden on his shoulders,' Wolff said, predicting lawmakers would 'do everything possible to frustrate him and to humiliate him and to make him pay for being Donald Trump.'He acknowledged there is an unresolved question over whether such an aggressive strategy is smart politics. He pointed out that previous efforts to punish Trump in Congress had 'turned out to be actually probably bad politics', a nod to the electoral backlash that can follow overreach.Wolff believes the damage to Donald Trump will not stop with Democrats. He argued that, in the second half of what he calls 'MAGA 2.0', Republicans themselves will become less inclined to fall in line.'His own party begins to move further and further away from him, and we're already seeing this now,' Wolff said, citing resistance to Trump's favoured Save America Act. According to Wolff, Republicans keep telling Trump 'we can't do it', a refusal he characterises as a 'clear rebuff.'Donald Trump and the JD Vance Warning SignWolff sees Vice-President JD Vance as the early test case of how Republican 2028 hopefuls might treat Donald Trump once the midterms are over.On a recent appearance on The Joe Rogan Experience, Vance said the Trump administration was 'guilty' of botching the release of files linked to Trump's long‑time associate, the convicted child sex trafficker Jeffrey Epstein.Wolff's reaction on the podcast was unfiltered. 'We saw JD Vance this past week go on Joe Rogan and, godd–, if he wasn't basically publicly proclaiming his distance from Donald Trump,' he told Coles.In Wolff's view, that kind of public daylight is not an accident. He contrasted Vance's approach with that of Kamala Harris in 2024, when she ran for president after Joe Biden withdrew.'During the 2024 race, everybody was saying she has got to get some more, put distance between herself and Joe Biden. And she really didn't,' Wolff said. 'But in this case, JD Vance was clearly putting distance between himself and Donald Trump. So, I think we will see that across the spectrum of 2028 contenders.'The underlying calculation, Wolff suggested, is harshly pragmatic. With Trump's approval rating at 37% and voters widely unhappy with both his economic record and his handling of the war in Iran, Republicans eyeing the White House have every incentive to say, politely or not, that they would do things differently.White House Fires Back at Michael Wolff Over Donald Trump ClaimsIt can be recalled that Wolff has long been a lightning rod for the Trump orbit. Officials have previously accused him of fabrication and bias in response to his reporting on the president's inner circle.In one extraordinary statement, White House Communications Director Steven Cheung said: 'Michael Wolff is a lying sack of s– and has been proven to be a fraud. He routinely fabricates stories originating from his sick and warped imagination, only possible because he has a severe and debilitating case of Trump Derangement Syndrome that has rotted his peanut‑sized brain.'As of this writing, the administration has not, however, directly rebutted his specific forecast about Congress turning into a 'noose' after the midterms, or about Republicans like Vance starting to peel off.Michael Wolff's latest broadside lands after a stretch in which Trump has openly fretted about Republican majorities slipping away and has tried to shape the rules of the vote itself.The journalist argued that Trump's anxiety has fuelled what he describes as a series of efforts to suppress turnout ahead of the midterm elections, alongside rhetoric justifying 'even further repressions' in that Thursday speech.Trump's critics frame those moves as an attempt to blunt a coming backlash over his handling of the economy and his war in Iran, while his supporters call them necessary safeguards.Wolff sees the coming months not just as another election cycle but as the start of Trump's long lame‑duck slide, even if he clings to office. In his telling, Congress will be more obstructive, Republican hopefuls like JD Vance will become more openly critical, and the protective shell that once surrounded Donald Trump will start to crack.
news.author: Jim Yango Fantonial

news.title: How a three-year-old software glitch at New Jersey's Motor Vehicle Commission led to registration of 6,600 noncitizens
news.text: New Jersey's Motor Vehicle Commission (MVC) faced a major software glitch three years ago. Due to this glitch, thousands of non-citizens were wrongly registered to vote in the state. Now, New Jersey Governor Mikie Sherrill has announced that the glitch caused 6,600 non-citizens in the state to be mistakenly registered as voters between June 2023 and June 2024, and roughly 400 of them voted. State officials said all those affected had self-identified as non-citizens when they applied for a driver’s license or identification card but were added to the voter rolls due to the software glitch.​According to a report by The New York Times, while announcing the findings, Sherrill said the registrations resulted from what she described as a "serious software error" linked to the MVC's voter registration system. The governor added that the affected individuals would be removed from the voter rolls. He said the state was working to protect them from possible legal or immigration-related consequences after they were registered "through no fault of their own."​The error was found and corrected in 2024 during the tenure of former Governor Phil Murphy, but it was not made public at the time, Sherrill said. Her administration officials learned of the matter last week and opened an investigation. The state has also hired an independent law firm to examine how the software failure occurred and said the findings of that review will be made public.​Software vendor blamed as state begins investigation​Sherrill said the software vendor, Idemia, was responsible for releasing the faulty system and confirmed that the company was already being phased out in New Jersey. However, Idemia said the state's Division of Elections was "ultimately responsible" for verifying voter eligibility after receiving information from the Motor Vehicle Commission.​“It’s unacceptable that the vendor released software with such a glaring error. It’s unacceptable that the M.V.C. took a year to get this issue fixed. And it’s unacceptable that no one in the previous administration brought this to light, demanded accountability or took action when it happened years ago,” Sherrill said.​Idemia, which says it provides technology services to more than 600 government agencies and has worked with New Jersey's MVC for four decades, is being replaced by Talus Software, according to Motor Vehicle Commission chief Rosalie Johnson.​Around 400 ballots cast, state says no election outcome was affected​According to the governor, there is "no evidence, at this time, that any elections were swayed," including the 2024 presidential election. If all 400 affected non-citizens voted in New Jersey's 2024 presidential election, they would account for about 0.009% of the state's total votes. Kamala Harris won New Jersey by more than 252,000 votes in that election. However, the New York Times report said it could not independently verify the figures released by the state.​The disclosure comes as US President Donald Trump and Republican lawmakers continue to push for the SAVE America Act, which would require documentary proof of citizenship for voter registration and place additional restrictions on mail voting. White House spokeswoman Abigail Jackson said, “Democrats and their media allies have repeatedly said it is impossible for noncitizens to register to vote, let alone cast a ballot. American voters deserve to have confidence that our elections are safe and secure.”​Joe Gruters, chairman of the Republican National Committee, also referred to the findings while urging Congress to pass the SAVE America Act. He said, “Democrats keep claiming this never happens, yet every new discovery tells the same old story. Enough excuses.”​David Becker, executive director of the nonpartisan Center for Election Innovation & Research, said administrative errors of this kind are uncommon and argued that state officials are best placed to resolve them.​"They have access to substantially better data than the federal government," adding that "noncitizen registration remains extremely rare, and noncitizen voting is infinitesimally rare."​New Jersey plans additional safeguards​Sherrill said New Jersey would continue its practice of not sharing personally identifying information of affected migrants with the federal government. She also said the New Jersey Innovation Authority is working to update electronic pin pads at MVC locations so voter registration information is displayed in five languages instead of only English.​"We will be looking at different possibilities of making our elections more secure going forward," Sherrill said.​In 2019, New Jersey passed legislation allowing undocumented immigrants to obtain driver’s licenses. Eligible residents can also register to vote when applying for or renewing driver’s licenses and non-driver identification cards at the Motor Vehicle Commission.Get the latest technology news and updates. Download the TOI App.

news.title: House Republicans fail to turn Trump’s transgender military ban into law as two members defect
news.text:
An amendment to codify President Donald Trump’s ban on transgender people serving in the U.S. military according to their gender identity failed to pass as an amendment to Congress’ annual defense bill after two Republicans defected.

Rep. Lauren Boebert (R-Colo.) proposed the amendment during a flurry of proposed amendments to the National Defense Authorization Act on the House floor on Tuesday evening. 

Last month, an appeals court in a 2-1 order struck down the Trump administration’s executive order, calling it fueled by “animus” and the disqualifications listed “completely unexplained and have no reasonable justification.”

The amendment failed 212-217. Reps. Mike Lawler of New York and Brian Fitzpatrick of Pennsylvania, both of whom represent districts that voted for Kamala Harris, opposed the amendment. 

Republican delegates James Moylan from Guam, and Kimberlyn King-Hinds from the Northern Mariana Islands, also opposed it, though delegates from commonwealths do not get a vote on the final passage of the NDAA.

But Rep. Nancy Mace (R-S.C.), the loudly anti-transgender Republican who lost her primary for governor, successfully proposed two anti-trans amendments. One amendment would ban TRICARE, the military health insurance plan, from “covering certain gender related medical procedures and treatments.” 

Five Democrats would join Republicans to pass the amendment 219-208: Reps. Shomari Figures of Alabama, Terri Sewell of Alabama, Henry Cuellar of Texas, Don Davis of North Carolina and Vicente Gonzalez of Texas. Davis, Cuellar and Gonzalez all represent districts that voted for Trump while Republicans plan to redraw their congressional map in Alabama, which could put Figures at risk.

Another amendment by Mace would prohibit transgender people assigned male at birth from participating in women’s K-12 sports at Department of Defense-run schools. That amendment would also pass 221-203 with Davis, Gonzalez, Cuelar, Sewell and Figures voting for it.

“Both of my NDAA amendments targeting mentally ill transgenders — and protecting our women and girls — passed the floor of the U.S. House of Representatives tonight with flying colors,” Mace said on social media. 

Mace, a member of the Armed Services Committee, had made anti-trans policy a hallmark of her third term in Congress as she teed up a run for governor. But she would place fifth last month in the Republican primary. She also passed on running for the Senate seat of the late Lindsey Graham. 

The NDAA sets national defense policy and has often been one of the only successful avenues for Republicans to successfully pass anti-trans provisions. In 2024, House Republicans inserted an amendment to ban TRICARE from covering “certain medical procedures for children that could result in sterilization.” The legislation eventually passed.

In 2025, Republicans successfully inserted an amendment that prohibited transgender athletes from participating in sports designated for women and girls at the United States Military Academy, the U.S. Naval Academy and the U.S. Air Force Academy. 

The Senate has yet to pass its version of the NDAA for the fiscal year of 2027. In the past, Democrats have been able to strip some amendments from the NDAA because of the fact it needs 60 votes to pass. 

The Senate Armed Services Committee has already completed its markup. If it passes, the two chambers will go to conference to negotiate a final version of the legislation.
news.summary: An amendment by Lauren Boebert fails, but two anti-trans amendments by Nancy Mace were passed late Tuesday
news.author: Eric Garcia

news.title: Fox News Power Rankings: Democrats lead in the House, but redistricting keeps the GOP in the game
news.text: The president’s party has lost an average of 25 U.S. House seats in Trump-era midterm elections. Given that Democrats only have an edge on the generic ballot and in party affiliation, so far, they look like they’re on track for an "average" night. Republican redistricting wins change that calculation. The GOP is likely to bank about a dozen seats from new district boundaries drawn by Republican-controlled state legislatures, up to nine of those in Florida and Texas alone. Democratic Gov. Gavin Newsom scored his party an extra three to five seats after voters approved his gerrymandering referendum, but overall, the GOP won this battle in what now looks like a redistricting "forever war." WHERE TRUMP, GOP VS DEMOCRATS REDISTRICTING BATTLE HEADS NEXT IN WAKE OF KEY COURT RULINGS Together, this adds up to a small Democratic lead in the House. They start with 211 races in their categories, or seven short of the 218 they need for a majority. If they win more than a handful of toss-up races, they will have enough to flip the chamber. There is enough room for the Republicans to maintain control on a good night for the party. Since toss-up races tend to be won and lost together, they could even improve on the slim majority they currently enjoy. Five races that tell the story Redistricting is at the heart of many key races this year, but the top of the ticket in each state, candidate quality and ground game are still important themes. Here are five districts to watch. New York’s 17th district The 17th district, up in the Hudson Valley, is the quintessential median district: a place that, on any given day, could swing in either direction. Voters elected Republican Mike Lawler here by 6 points in 2024, but also voted for former Vice President Kamala Harris by 0.3 points, making it one of the rare ‘crossover’ districts of that election, and arguably the top target for Democrats in a more favorable political environment. The party got moderate and combat veteran Cait Conley through the primary, despite competition from a progressive challenger, and she’s charted a center-left, anti-Trump course to flip the district. She’s one of the top 10 fundraisers in competitive districts this quarter. Lawler won’t go down without a fight. He has Trump’s endorsement but disagrees with him at times, including over his refusal to sign a bipartisan housing bill. This is a competitive race in a blue-leaning year, so it starts at Lean D. Pennsylvania’s 7th district Democrats fought hard to elect Gov. Josh Shapiro in Pennsylvania in the last midterms. This time, they’re in the driver’s seat. Shapiro has the support of 53% of voters in a July poll, and the GOP isn’t likely to invest in flipping it. The House is a different story. The Keystone State is tied only with California for the largest number of highly competitive districts: four across the state, all on the east side. MIKE JOHNSON PREDICTS GOP COULD GAIN UP TO EIGHT SEATS IN MIDTERMS, DEFYING HISTORICAL TRENDS The seventh district is notable for its candidates: Rep. Ryan Mackenzie, the Republican who flipped the district in 2024 and whose mother, Milou, is a popular state representative in the Lehigh Valley, and Bob Brooks, a local firefighter who enjoys the endorsements of both Shapiro and Senator Bernie Sanders. The race offers a clear look at whether a Democrat who seems to have unified both wings of his party can also appeal to White working-class voters who have swung toward the GOP during the Trump era. It’s a toss-up. North Carolina’s 1st district Republicans took a knife to North Carolina’s district boundaries last year to make this seat, in the northeast corner of the state, more Republican. Under the new lines, the district has fewer Black voters, and that includes the seat’s current congressman, Rep. Don Davis, a moderate Democrat whose home in Snow Hill was moved to the 3rd district. Davis is still running in the first district these midterms (congresspeople don’t have to live in the districts they represent), where he will face Republican and retired Army Colonel Laurie Buckhout, a pro-Trump conservative who is leaning into her military experience. It’s a close race by many measures, including fundraising, with both candidates raising $1 million in the last quarter. Republicans will flip the seat on a good night, to be sure. But in what looks like a favorable cycle for Democrats, it’s not yet clear that the GOP has an edge. It starts as a toss-up. Alabama’s 2nd district Alabama’s second district is currently home to Rep. Shomari Figures, a Democrat who flipped the seat for the first time in over a decade after a federal court ordered the state to redraw it with a higher Black population in 2023. He probably won’t occupy it much longer. After the Supreme Court’s ruling to narrow a key provision of the Voting Rights Act in April, Gov. Kay Ivey restored the original map; White voters now make up nearly two-thirds of the district’s population. Figures is still running; he’s likely to face Trump-endorsed state Rep. Rhett Marques in the general election in a race that the National Republican Congressional Committee is investing in. It’s a redistricting win for Republicans, and a Likely R pickup in the forecast. Florida’s 14th district Other redistricting efforts might not yield such clear-cut results. In Florida, Gov. Ron DeSantis’ new map could yield up to four more Republicans in November, but it depends on what kind of night the GOP has across the country. As the Wall Street Journal pointed out in April, "making a blue district more Republican means making a red one less Republican." (Florida’s 26th district is a great example of the latter, though it’s still ranked Solid R in this forecast.) The delicate balance has also meant creating new Democratic-incumbent districts that, yes, have more GOP-leaning voters in them, but not so many as to guarantee a flip. Florida’s 14th district is a perfect case study. Under its new lines, it would’ve voted for President Trump by 11 points in 2024. Given Florida’s rightward drift, it would usually start as a likely GOP pickup. That’s not the case this year. Incumbent Democrat Kathy Castor doesn’t have a competitor yet, but she has $1.5 million in cash on hand and support from the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee, so she’s not out of this fight. It’s a Lean R race. CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD THE FOX NEWS APP Tomorrow, the full governor’s forecast, with rankings for Iowa, Michigan, Georgia, Nevada, Ohio, Wisconsin and 30 other states.
news.summary: Republican redistricting wins could bank a dozen seats, but Democrats start just seven short of 218 with toss-up races poised to decide control.
news.author: Rémy Numa

news.title: Trump biographer reveals the trouble he doesn’t see coming after the midterms
news.text:
President Donald Trump is in for a miserable time after the forthcoming midterm elections, but according to his one-time biographer, there is another hardship he cannot see that "will become an incredible noose around his neck."

Michael Wolff is a longtime reporter and author, best known for his extensive coverage of Trump's political career, including several books about his chaotic first term. On the latest episode of his Daily Beast podcast, "Inside Trump's Head," Wolff said that Trump is in for a rough time serving out the rest of his term after the midterms, in which Democrats are widely expected to at least retake the House majority. 

"They will do everything possible to frustrate him and to humiliate him and to make him pay for being Donald Trump," Wolff explained. "And that may be good politics or bad politics. That’s unclear because they’ve done this before and it turned out to be actually probably bad politics.”

He also argued that he will be in for an unexpected difficulty once those pivotal races wrap up: his own party.

“But the other thing is, in terms of being a lame duck, is that things happen in his own party. So his own party begins to move further and further away from him, and we’re already seeing this now,” Wolff added, noting the GOP's current inability to pass the SAVE America Act. “They keep telling him, ‘We can’t do it,’ but for whatever reason, that is not going to happen. That’s a clear rebuff.”

The SAVE Act is an election reform bill that Trump has repeatedly called his number one priority for Congress. If signed into law, the bill would impose new rules requiring Americans to present certain proof of citizenship when registering to vote, a move that critics have said would disenfranchise millions of voters without easy access to their birth certificates or passports. Despite his relentless push for it, Republicans in the Senate have shot down the possibility, stressing that it does not have the support needed to overcome the filibuster.

Wolff also said that, in the wake of the 2026 midterms, Trump will start to see Republican 2028 hopefuls begin to distance themselves from him, in an effort to avoid being too heavily associated with his unpopular agenda.

“We saw [Vice President] JD Vance this past week go on Joe Rogan and — godd——, if he wasn’t basically publicly proclaiming his distance from Donald Trump," Wolff said.

He added: “During the 2024 race, everybody was saying [Kamala Harris] has got to get some more — put distance between herself and Joe Biden. And she really didn’t. But in this case, JD Vance was clearly putting distance between himself and Donald Trump. So, I think we will see that across the spectrum of 2028 contenders.”

news.title: Kalshi events contracts blocked by Washington State judge
news.text:
Washington State’s request to block the prediction market, Kalshi, has been granted by a King County Superior Court Judge due to the platform potentially breaking state gambling laws. 

Kalshi rose to prominence as a prediction market platform that allows users to bet on the outcome of a wide variety of outcomes like sports events, elections and even what people will say during public appearances. It was famously more accurate than pollsters in predicting Donald Trump’s 2024 election victory over Kamala Harris. 

There is currently an ongoing feud between state and federal government over who has jurisdiction to oversee these prediction platforms with the US Commodity Futures Trading Commission claiming exclusive oversight, whereas some state governments are seeing it as an illegal form of gambling, classifying Kalshi as an online sportsbook.

That is how Washington and the judge, John McHale, saw it when he granted a preliminary injunction, preventing any further Kalshi events contracts in the state. Judge McHale said that they showed “a likelihood of actual and substantial injury ​to Washington consumers from illegal gambling activities.” 

The judge said he plans to issue on order implementing the injunction on 5 August, and the Washington Attorney General believes this is an important first step in regulating his new form of betting. 

“Kalshi padded their pockets as they promoted illegal betting on sports, elections, the total number of ​measles cases this year, ​what will witnesses ⁠say during a child trafficking hearing, and even natural disasters,” Washington Attorney General Nick Brown said in a statement. “This victory is ​the first step toward holding Kalshi accountable.”

Massachusetts, Nevada, Michigan and New York have all won similar court cases recently, blocking Kalshi from operating their prediction markets, but it is not all one -ay traffic as the federal appeals court cited the CTFC’s authority to prevent New Jersey from regulating Kalshi’s sports events contracts which the platform believes should have been the case in Washington as well, as the issue continues to divide courts. 

“States don’t have jurisdiction to regulate prediction markets,” Kalshi said ‌in ⁠a statement. “We’re disappointed to see Washington state continue wasting taxpayer dollars.”
news.summary: Washington State win court case to block Kalshi operating in the region
