news.title: William Lawrence Is Running in a Purple Michigan District — on His Pro-Palestine Credentials
news.text:
In key swing districts across the country, Democratic Party leaders have backed centrist candidates with carefully calibrated positions on charged issues such as artificial intelligence and the Israeli-Palestinian conflict.

William Lawrence is running a very different campaign.

In a purple congressional district in Michigan, Lawrence, a co-founder of the progressive Sunrise Movement, is calling for a moratorium on new data centers and labeling Israel’s war in Gaza a genocide.

The approach mirrors that of Michigan Democratic Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed, who has endorsed Lawrence.

Both men are making strong showings in the polls, and now the centrists are fighting back. A shadowy group is running misleading ads that question Lawrence’s progressive bona fides.

The line of attack is telling, Lawrence told The Intercept.

“People are sick of the status quo,” he said. “We have seen this in many cases in other elections this cycle — that corporate centrists are attacking progressives for not being progressive enough. And it shows you what voters are hungry for is change.”

An unabashed progressive, Lawrence breaks the mold of the typical candidate to run for a seat like Michigan’s 7th Congressional District, which is centered around the state capital of Lansing and the college town of East Lansing.

The district was previously represented by centrist Democrat Elissa Slotkin, who now serves in the Senate, and is currently held by Republican first-term Rep. Tom Barrett.

The other two candidates running in Michigan’s August 4 primary hew closer to Slotkin’s mold. Bridget Brink is a former U.S. ambassador to Ukraine who quit in protest of President Donald Trump’s policies on the war with Russia, and Matt Maasdam is a former Navy SEAL who served as a military aide to President Barack Obama.

Unlike Lawrence, both declined to call Israel’s war on Gaza genocide at a debate.

Lawrence also comes from a starkly different background: While Brink and Maasdam traveled the corridors of power in Washington, Lawrence focused on building grassroots power, first through his role co-founding the youth-led Sunrise Movement, which backed the Green New Deal, and more recently helping found the Michigan Rent Is Too Damn High coalition.

Mutual Endorsements

On July 7, Lawrence and El-Sayed announced that they were endorsing each other. Like Lawrence, El-Sayed is running in the progressive lane in his primary while arguing that it will not hurt his electability in the general.

“We have a lot of people who want to back both of us,” Lawrence said. “Given that the two of us both prioritize having a very robust, grassroots campaign rather than just relying on tens of millions of dollars in dark money like our respective opponents, it is especially beneficial for us to be able to integrate our grassroots operations.”

Although no independent polls of the race have been released, Lawrence’s campaign commissioned one showing him leading the pack. Another commissioned by Brink’s campaign showed her ahead.

Now that Lawrence has demonstrated that he has a shot at winning, the attacks have begun.

The Congressional Black Caucus is targeting him over a podcast episode in which Lawrence remarked to a Black anti-war activist during a conversation on organizing that he was frustrated with the position of some older Black political leaders backing “capitalist, imperialist American power.”

Lawrence says his comments have been misrepresented while apologizing for his choice of words.

Meanwhile, a super PAC called the Crush MAGA PAC is targeting Lawrence, rather than the Republican holding the seat. The group has plowed more than $500,000 into a highly misleading ad that accuses Lawrence of investing in “Wall Street, big oil, and data centers” and campaigning against Democrats for supporting the 2024 Uncommitted movement, which sought to pressure party candidates over Israel’s war on Gaza.

The investments highlighted in the anti-Lawrence ad make up a small portion of the $11,000 Lawrence has invested in a mutual fund; the mutual fund, not Lawrence himself, chose them. And, for his part, Lawrence eventually supported Kamala Harris in the 2024 election.

The source of the ad campaign against Lawrence is unclear, but he has speculated that it may be powered by pro-Israel donors.

Crush MAGA PAC is affiliated with another group called the Save Democracy PAC, which has received money from the American Israel Public Affairs Committee’s super PAC, United Democracy Project. United Democracy Project, however, denied getting involved in the Michigan congressional race. (Neither Crush MAGA PAC nor Save Democracy PAC responded to requests for comment.)

If Lawrence succeeds in the primary, he will have to win in a general election in a purple district. He believes he can show that progressive candidates can win in split districts, pointing to the election and reelection of Congressional Progressive Caucus member Rep. Chris Deluzio in a swing district in Pennsylvania.

“He’s not throwing people under the bus, and he has joined the progressive caucus, and he has now turned that into a more comfortable district. He has been winning with healthy margins,” Lawrence said. “That is what I hope to be able to do as well.”
news.author: Matt Sledge

news.title: Everyone Comparing Maine to 2024 Is Looking at the Wrong State | Opinion
news.text: Two years ago, I participated in one of the most controversial decisions either major political party has made in modern American history. As a delegate to the Democratic National Convention, I voted to replace President Joe Biden with Kamala Harris as our party’s nominee following concerns over Biden’s age and health. I believed the circumstances justified it, and I still do. But I also understand why millions of Americans were uncomfortable with what they watched: primary voters had selected one nominee, Biden, and delegates selected another. However urgent many Democrats believed the substitution to be, it felt to a great many Americans like something that had happened to them, rather than by them. That experience is why I have watched the reaction to Graham Platner’s withdrawal from Maine's Senate race with particular interest. Since Platner stepped aside following a sexual assault allegation he denies, Republicans and cable commentators have repeatedly invoked 2024, arguing that Democrats are once again overriding the will of their own primary voters. Having participated in the original, I think critics have drawn the wrong lesson from 2024. Moreover, if there is a 2024 redux on the horizon, it appears to be unfolding in another state, South Carolina. The defining problem in 2024 was never that delegates cast the deciding votes. Political parties maintain rules for extraordinary circumstances because extraordinary circumstances happen: presidents withdraw, candidates die, scandals end campaigns weeks before elections. The political damage came from the choreography. Within days of Biden’s withdrawal, governors, senators, donors and party leaders converged around a single successor, and millions of Americans reasonably concluded that the outcome had been settled before delegates ever gathered. The formal vote occurred and the rules were followed, but a process that was deliberative on paper felt, to much of the country, like a coronation. That distinction matters because political legitimacy rests on more than procedural compliance. It rests on whether citizens believe meaningful competition still exists at the moment a choice is made in their name. Measured against that standard, Maine looks nothing like 2024. To be maximally clear about what is fair in the comparison: Platner won his primary decisively in June, his withdrawal means Democratic voters will not directly choose the nominee who faces Republican incumbent Susan Collins in November, and the vacancy will instead be filled through the party process established under state law. That deserves scrutiny, and Democrats should not pretend otherwise. But the similarity ends there. Unlike the rapid consolidation that followed Biden's withdrawal in 2024, Maine Democrats opened the process to multiple candidates and held county nominating meetings across all 16 counties to select 500 delegates for a special convention. Those delegates—not national party leaders—will cast the decisive votes, and while former Maine Senate President Troy Jackson has emerged as the front-runner after winning support from much of Platner’s political coalition, he did so by competing for delegate support rather than through an elite party coronation. It is not a perfect substitute for a primary, but it is a process that sought to preserve competition, transparency and legitimacy under extraordinarily compressed circumstances. Now consider what Republicans have constructed in South Carolina following the shocking death of Senator Lindsey Graham. South Carolina Governor Henry McMaster immediately appointed Graham’s sister, Darline Graham Nordone, to the seat to finish out her brother’s term. Despite Nordone having no political experience, Senator Tim Scott then promptly suggested Nordone become the permanent successor to the seat. President Donald Trump quickly followed, offering his “complete and total endorsement” to her for the full term before the filing period for the special primary had even opened. Several other Republicans, including Nancy Mace, Ralph Norman, William Timmons and Pamela Evette, had each been discussed as candidates or expressed interest in running. Yet, while the primary remains open as a matter of law, every ambitious Republican in the state has received the message—the decision has been made. This is precisely the dynamic Republicans have spent the past two years condemning. Their argument, repeated endlessly after 2024, was that Democrats had denied voters a genuine choice—that powerful people settled the outcome before anyone else had a meaningful opportunity to compete. They were not entirely wrong. Whatever else explains the Democrats’ 2024 defeat, many Americans came away believing they had watched party leaders manage an outcome rather than facilitate a decision, and in a country already deeply distrustful of its institutions, that perception carried real electoral consequences. Republicans understood this well enough to campaign on it. They appear not to have understood it well enough to avoid repeating the same mistake—on a faster timeline, on behalf of a family member with no experience, in a state whose voters will be asked to ratify the arrangement within weeks. None of this means South Carolina Republicans have broken any rules, and none of it means Maine Democrats have built a flawless process. The distinction between the two states is not procedural but perceptual, and it is perception that decides elections. A party convention can feel legitimate when delegates genuinely choose among competing candidates and so too can a primary feel hollow when its most important decision was made at the White House before the funeral. Voters proved in 2024 that they know the difference, and that they punish the parties who confuse managing an outcome with earning one. Republicans are entitled to keep calling Maine undemocratic. But until they can explain why a contested convention threatens democracy while a cleared field honors it, the comparison to 2024 will keep pointing somewhere they do not want it to—at a Senate seat in South Carolina being handed down like an inheritance, and at a party that learned nothing from the backlash it spent two years exploiting. Kaivan Shroff is a culture and politics commentator and attorney. He also serves as senior adviser to the D.C. nonprofit Institute for Education, was a 2024 DNC presidential delegate and worked as a digital organizer on Hillary Clinton's 2016 campaign. The views expressed in this article are the writer's own.
news.summary: "Every ambitious Republican in the state has received the message—the decision has been made."
news.author: Kaivan Shroff

news.title: Actually, no. Kamala Harris doesn't have a shot in 2028.
news.text:
There's an idea taking hold on the left that former Vice President Kamala Harris is a viable choice for the Democratic presidential nomination in 2028. She isn't. And even if she somehow won it, she'd be a certain loser in the general election.

A recent New York magazine piece by David Freedlander was headlined "Like It or Not, Kamala Harris Is the Front-runner." Her advisers now treat it as an article of faith that the compressed campaign cost her the chance to define herself in 2024.

If anything, it did her a favor. The 107 days between President Joe Biden's exit and Election Day, the same "107 Days" she titled her memoir after, meant no real primary and less time having to defend an unpopular administration. She got to run as a blank slate instead of a scrutinized nominee. The thing her team calls a handicap was actually her cover.

She's leading early primary polls by a wide margin. One recent survey had her at 36%, more than triple her nearest rival. But that's mainly about name recognition. 

Prediction markets, where people put real money on the outcome instead of just answering a pollster's phone call, have her far lower.

Kamala Harris' problem, in one sentence

In Freedlander's conversations with Democratic insiders, the stale metaphors arrive early, which is never a good sign. "You don't have to be a rocket scientist to understand that right now we need leaders that can take us in a different direction," says Minyon Moore, a Democratic operative.

Then this, from an unnamed supporter convinced that Harris is running: "She is looking for where the wave is going and is determined to ride it."

That's Harris' whole problem in one sentence. Donald Trump didn't ride any waves. Neither did Barack Obama. They were the waves. Voters can tell the difference between someone who generates energy and someone searching for a current to ride.

The same supporter: "Her mindset is that I should have won, and I am going to do it my way. … It was all these consultants … it was their fault."

What was "her way," exactly? Nobody says. But it's clear that the border, the economy and taxpayer-funded gender surgery for inmates hurt Democrats badly – and Harris doubling down on all of it, even as the race slipped away, hurt her specifically. Her former staffers blame the money and the ground game instead. Of course they do.

"People are looking forward," says Laura Telerski, a New Hampshire state rep who's met Harris but hasn't endorsed her. "I don't think she's bogged down in yesterday." 

"Look how quickly voters forgot about Trump's first term," adds an unnamed Democratic official.

In other words, they're betting Americans forget how the Biden-Harris years actually went.

But voters didn't return to Trump because they forgot who he was. They went back because they remembered the economy was better and the border was under control. Harris' "fascist dictatorship" routine didn't square with people's memories of a Trump term that had, in fact, been just fine.

One anonymous former aide bragged that Harris "comes in with 99% name ID, and everyone else is going to have to raise money and buy it." 

True, but total name recognition just means there's no room left to grow, and the former president's favorability has sat underwater since the day she left office.

What Democratic voters actually want in 2028

If Trump's rise taught Democrats anything, it's that voters will forgive plenty of rough edges for the real thing, which is why candidates like Rep. Jasmine Crockett of Texas, Sen. John Fetterman of Pennsylvania and Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez of New York have drawn the party's energy in recent years. 

It's why Democrats are gravitating toward candidates who come across as raw and unfiltered rather than manufactured and stage-managed.

The problem with Harris isn't that she's too moderate or too far left. She can lurch left as easily as she's lurched everywhere else. Her problem is that she isn't anything in particular: not authentic, not much of a populist, certainly not working-class and not even a committed democratic socialist. She'll be whomever the base needs, and the quotes above prove it.

Losing nominees rarely get a second act. Harris might be the exception for a simple reason: She's still technically first in line in the Democratic Party until she says otherwise. As a Black Asian woman, she's even more protected. Democrats handed her the nomination in 2024 rather than be seen passing over the first minority woman vice president for another White man. The same thinking that made her impossible to skip in 2024 makes her nearly impossible to skip past now, unless she rules herself out first.

In the magazine story, Freedlander admits a contest against Harris "could get messy," because women and voters of color anchor the Democratic coalition, and being seen defending those groups matters to the party's identity as much as winning does.

When he asked Democratic donors and operatives what they make of a "Kamala 2.0," a couple were candid. "I hope she runs, because then she will lose and we will never have to hear from her again," says Democratic strategist Alex Hoffman. "She will never win an election at a national scale. Never."

But most gave Freedlander a particularly damning response: a heavy sigh, followed by a long pause. Everyone knows exactly what that pause means, but nobody can say it out loud.

Daniel Allott, USA TODAY's conservative opinion editor, is author of the book "On the Road in Trump's America: A Journey Into the Heart of a Divided Nation."

John Fetterman of Pennsylvania and Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez of New York have drawn the party's energy in recent years.

It's why Democrats are gravitating toward candidates who come across as raw and unfiltered rather than manufactured and stage-managed.

The problem with Harris isn't that she’s too moderate or too far left. She can lurch left as easily as she's lurched everywhere else. Her problem is that she isn't anything in particular: not authentic, not much of a populist, certainly not working-class and not even a committed democratic socialist. She’ll be whomever the base needs, and the quotes above prove it.

Losing nominees rarely get a second act. Harris might be the exception for a simple reason: She’s still technically first in line in the Democratic Party until she says otherwise. As a Black Asian woman, she’s even more protected. Democrats handed her the nomination in 2024 rather than be seen passing over the first minority woman vice president for another White man. The same thinking that made her impossible to skip in 2024 makes her nearly impossible to skip past now, unless she rules herself out first.

In the magazine story, Freedlander admits a contest against Harris “could get messy,” because women and voters of color anchor the Democratic coalition, and being seen defending those groups matters to the party’s identity as much as winning does.

When he asked Democratic donors and operatives what they make of a “Kamala 2.0,” a couple were candid. “I hope she runs, because then she will lose and we will never have to hear from her again,” says Democratic strategist Alex Hoffman. “She will never win an election at a national scale. Never.”

But most gave Freedlander a particularly damning response: a heavy sigh, followed by a long pause. Everyone knows exactly what that pause means, but nobody can say it out loud.

Daniel Allott, USA TODAY’s conservative opinion editor, is author of the book “On the Road in Trump’s America: A Journey Into the Heart of a Divided Nation.”
news.author: Usa Today

news.title: Arizona Republicans nominate MAGA stalwarts: 5 primary takeaways
news.text:
Rep. Andy Biggs sailed to the Republican nomination for Arizona governor July 21, further solidifying President Donald Trump's nearly perfect record in the 2026 GOP primaries.

Now comes the more difficult task for the five-term conservative firebrand in his quest to defeat Democratic Gov. Katie Hobbs: winning over needed independents without straying too far from the White House.

Arizona is one of the most hotly contested swing states in the country, but in several Republican primaries this year the experts have warned Trump-endorsed contenders will have a tougher time in the general election.

Even some of Biggs' fellow Republicans cast the former House Freedom Caucus chair as too extreme to prevail over Hobbs.

The governor's race had been rated as a "toss-up" by experts, but it's been tilted more in Hobbs' favor given Biggs' polarizing views, including his skepticism President Joe Biden did not win the 2020 election.

The Grand Canyon State will also be a roadmap for controlling the U.S. House of Representatives, where at least three Republican-held seats are being targeted by Democrats.

Alex Kolodin, a state representative from Scottsdale, seized the GOP nomination for secretary of state. He's received widespread attention for claiming that U.S. elections, including those in Arizona, are unfair and "rigged."

And in a sign of the Trump administration's intent to spotlight concerns about voter integrity in the key swing state this fall, the Department of Justice sent federal monitors into the state on July 21.

Here are the main takeaways from Tuesday's contest.

Biggs pivots ahead of closely watched governor's race

Like many GOP primary victors this year Biggs benefited from proximity to Trump along with having one of the most conservative voting records on Capitol Hill.

"I hate to lose him from Congress. You know, I'm sort of torn with that," Trump said during a Phoenix rally hosted by Turning Point USA in April. "I hate to lose him, but he'll be a great governor."

That loyalty extended to the five-term congressman voting against certifying the 2020 election results, but as polls showed Biggs easily winning the primary he began to pivot weeks ago in an attempt to win over independents and skeptical Republicans.

In an interview ahead of the July 21 contest, Biggs emphasized working with the late Sen. John McCain on closing an Amber Alert system loophole for Native American tribes. He also cites is another 2018 proposal he co-sponsored with Rep. Hakeem Jeffries, D-New York, now the Democrats' minority leader, to require more reporting from lobbyists.

"I do have a record of of reaching across the aisle and working with people to get important things done," Biggs told USA TODAY.

Hobbs, Democrats plan anti-Trump avalanche ahead of November

The Biggs' campaign said in a strategy memo that it believes Hobbs is "uniquely vulnerable," given the razor-thin margin she won the office in 2022.

But as much as the president and his allies hope they can catapult a swing state Democratic governor out office, forecasters have shifted their predictions from "toss-up" to "leans Democratic."

Biggs' role as a major player in the fake elector scheme in 2020 will likely be re-litigated during this election, which is setting up to be a major referendum on the president, whose unpopular initiatives − such as launching a war with Iran that has driven up gas prices − Biggs has supported.

"I know this race is going to be close no matter what, because Arizona is a swing state," Hobbs, who won by less than one percentage point in 2022, told USA TODAY. "I'm going to earn every vote by showing Arizonans how I'm putting what’s best for the state ahead of partisan politics – lowering costs, securing the border, and bringing good-paying jobs here – and that's how I’ll win this race."

DOJ dispatches federal election monitors

As Arizona voters were flocking to the ballot box, the Trump administration announced it was sending election "monitors" into polling sites in three counties on the lookout for election fraud.

"All citizens and only citizens should get out and vote in federal races today, feeling confident that the government is here to help," Assistant Attorney General for Civil Rights Harmeet Dhillon said in a July 21 post on X.

Christopher Weber, director of the Voter Project at the University of Arizona, described the DOJ's decision to send election monitors into a state during an active primary as "highly unusual."

But Bill Gates, executive director of Arizona State University's Mechanics of Democracy Laboratory, said the practice was "nothing new."

"Their presence has been a routine part of elections under administrations of both parties and has been intended to help ensure compliance with federal voting rights laws," he said.

The Justice Department's decision mirrored one made during the Biden administration when monitors were sent into four counties during the 2024 presidential election. Arizona elections officials told the USA TODAY Network shortly before polls closed that it was "business as usual," and that nothing out of the ordinary had been reported to them.

But the step also comes at a time when Democrats and their allies are increasingly nervous about Trump's demands to overhaul of U.S. elections before the midterms and ongoing false claims about widespread election fraud.

In a prime-time address this month, the president made sweeping, inaccurate allegations about broad vulnerabilities with voting systems. Trump exaggerated the level of interference China made into the 2020 election, but mostly used the speech as a rallying cry for Congress to pass the SAVE America Act, a robust voting bill that would require proof of citizenship upon registering to vote.

Arizona is the only state that requires proof of citizenship to register to vote in local and statewide elections, which it installed through a ballot initiative in 2004. Yet in a July 7 letter to Arizona's secretary of state, Dhillon threatened prosecution to any election official who "knowingly retains noncitizens" on the state's voter rolls.

Democrats flipping Congress runs through Arizona

Each week the president's approval ratings appear to be getting weaker and weaker, with just 36% of Americans approving of his job performance in a new Economist/YouGov poll released this week.

But that doesn't guarantee good fortune for Democrats this fall. A Washington Post-Ipsos poll from July found they have just a 48%-to-45% over Republicans on which party voters favor in the upcoming congressional elections.

If the GOP wants to holds its slim House majority, that will mean defending many battleground seats and three in Arizona are being targeted by Democrats. Chief among them being Arizona's 1st congressional district election to replace Republican David Schweikert, who lost the gubernatorial primary Tuesday to Biggs. 

Trump won that district -- covering northeastern Maricopa County -- by three percentage points in 2024, but Biden won it by one and half points in 2020.

The president's choice is Republican Jay Feely, a former NFL Pro Bowl kicker seeking to capitalize on his name ID that helped him defeat former state Rep. Joe Chaplik and businessman John Trobough.

Democrats are choosing between former state legislator Amish Shah and Marlene Galán-Woods, a former TV news anchor endorsed by the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee, the House Democrat's political arm. But with about 76% of precincts, Shah is leading Galan-Woods by roughly 10 percentage points.

The DCCC also lists Arizona's 6th Congressional District as a possible flip, where it hopes to unseat Republican Rep. Juan Ciscomani, whose district Trump carried by less than one percentage point in 2024. JoAnna Mendoza, a Marine Corps veteran, ran unopposed in the Democratic primary for that seat.

Arizona's 2nd District, currently represented by Republican Rep. Eli Crane, is also a Democratic target. Experts are less optimistic about their chances there, given Trump won the area by 15 percentage points two years ago. But political observers point out Democrat Jonathan Nez, a former Navajo Nation president, did outperform former Vice President Kamala Harris in the district.

It is also noteworthy that amid a rising tide of more progressive Democrats defeating longtime incumbents, four-term Rep. Greg Stanton trounced activist Kai Newkirk, who leaned into opposing data centers drawing the ire from big-money AI groups.

2020 election conspiracies re-emerge in Arizona secretary of state race

Arizona's secretary of state will be a critical post in the 2028 presidential race as the chief official responsible for maintaining the voter registration database and overseeing elections.

That fall contest will once again be a proxy war about the 2020 contest, now that Republicans have chosen state Rep. Alex Kolodin, a Scottsdale attorney, as their nominee.

Kolodin was part of a legal team that sought to throw out mail-in ballots and stop Arizona's 11 electors from casting votes for Biden. He was sanctioned in 2023 by the state bar for his role in those suits. As a state legislator he has offered up tighter voting rules, such as abolishing the state's early-voter list.

Secretary of State Adrian Fontes, the Democratic incumbent, defeated Republican Mark Finchem, a prominent Trump support who denied Biden's win, by more than 4 percentage points in 2022.

During that race, Fontes ran as outspoken "democracy defender," and has criticized Trump's ongoing fixation on that election six years ago while resisting the administration's multiple efforts to obtain Arizona's voter rolls.

Contributing: Josh Meyer, Ronald J. Hansen, Drew Pittock
news.author: Usa Today

news.title: Trump's Threats To Yank Broadcast Licenses Are Part of a Speech-Chilling Intimidation Campaign
news.text:
Imagine how Republicans would react if a Democratic president reflexively threatened to yank the broadcast licenses of TV stations because he did not like their news coverage or programming decisions. President Donald Trump has been doing that since his first term, and he did it again last week after ABC and NBC declined to offer live coverage of his speech about election security.

These threats are part of an intimidation campaign that has already yielded striking concessions to Trump's whims. His weaponization of the Federal Communications Commission (FCC) is designed to chill constitutionally protected speech, establishing a precedent that Republicans may regret when their opponents take control of the White House.

Brendan Carr, the Trump-appointed FCC chairman, seems to think broadcasters have a legal obligation to please the president. "Broadcasters must operate in the public interest," Carr declared in March, "and they will lose their licenses if they do not."

As Carr understands it, "the public interest" requires broadcasters to cover the president "fully, accurately, and fairly." It may also require them to eschew war reporting that embarrasses the president and punish late-night comedians whose jokes offend him.

Any attempt to impose such a vague, open-ended standard on speakers in other media would be plainly unconstitutional. But broadcasters are different, Carr avers, because "the American people have subsidized [them] to the tune of billions of dollars by providing free access to the nation's airwaves."

Trump echoed that rationale for speech regulation last Thursday, when he said broadcasters "use our public, multibillion-dollar-in-value airways for absolutely no money." But that is plainly not true, since broadcast licenses figure in the prices that media companies pay when they acquire TV or radio stations.

Trump nevertheless relied on that false premise in arguing that the failure of ABC and NBC to give his speech the attention he thought it deserved "should mean a revocation of their licenses." Whether or not Carr follows through on that suggestion, he has made it clear that he views correcting anti-Trump bias as an important part of his job.

That understanding of the FCC's mission explains why Carr seriously entertained the possibility that CBS committed "broadcast news distortion" by editing a pre-election interview with Kamala Harris in a way that made her seem slightly more cogent. It explains why Carr bragged about requiring changes to journalistic practices at CBS as a condition for approving Skydance Media's acquisition of Paramount, the network's parent company.

Carr's conception of "the public interest" was also at the root of his most flagrant attempt to exert control over broadcast content: the regulatory threats that preceded ABC's suspension of Jimmy Kimmel Live! last September. It likewise underlies his warning that talk shows like The View can no longer rely on their longstanding exemption from the "equal time" rule—a change that would effectively prohibit them from interviewing political candidates.

So far, Carr has not delivered on Trump's oft-repeated threats to revoke broadcast licenses. But last April, he announced an early review of ABC's licenses, ostensibly because of concerns about "unlawful discrimination."

Actual license revocations are not necessary to cow broadcasters, as illustrated by what happened after Trump filed a ludicrous lawsuit alleging that the Harris interview qualified as consumer fraud under Texas and federal law. Although CBS accurately noted that Trump's complaint was "completely without merit" and vowed to "vigorously defend against it," Paramount ultimately agreed to a $16 million settlement.

That decision followed Trump's threats to revoke the network's broadcast licenses and Carr's initiation of the "news distortion" investigation. It came as the FCC was deciding whether to allow the Paramount/Skydance merger, which it finally approved a few weeks later based on the expectation of a Trump-friendlier CBS News.

That episode showed how easily "the public interest" can be conflated with the president's personal interests. It's a lesson that Republicans should take to heart if they allow for the possibility that they will not always control these levers of power.

news.summary: FCC Chairman Brendan Carr seems to think TV stations are legally obligated to please the president.
news.author: Jacob Sullum

news.title: Rep. Biggs Wins GOP Primary, Will Challenge Hobbs for Arizona Governor in November
news.text:
PHOENIX—Incumbent Democratic Gov. Katie Hobbs will face Rep. Andy Biggs (R-Ariz.) in Arizona’s gubernatorial election this fall.

Biggs, who has served in the U.S. House since 2017, won the Republican primary in a landslide on July 21, defeating three other long-shot Republican candidates.

“I am deeply honored to be chosen by Arizona Republicans as their nominee to defeat Katie Hobbs in November,” Biggs wrote in a social media post on Tuesday night.

“This campaign has been driven by our grassroots supporters from the day we launched in January 2025 with a clear mission of Restoring the American Dream in our great state. Tonight’s results show that Arizonans overwhelmingly believe in our vision and I’m grateful to have earned their support.”

Biggs was not at his watch party inside of Dillon Precision in Scottsdale, Arizona, when his campaign initially declared victory to a packed room of supporters because bad weather caused a series of flight delays, according to his wife.

The congressman arrived at his victory party just before 2:30 a.m. ET (11:30 p.m. local time) to a crowd of a few dozen enthusiastic supporters chanting “Andy, Andy, Andy,” as he walked up to the stage.

Biggs started his speech thanking God and then his supporters.

“I’m so grateful to have the support of all of you, and I’m thankful that everybody who voted today, got out and voted,” Biggs added. “I was overwhelmed when I started seeing those vote totals come in. It’s awesome! Thank you for all the work you did!”

The Associated Press called the race around 11:03 p.m ET.

Hobbs, a former social worker who served as a state legislator and as Arizona secretary of state, ran unopposed in the Democratic primary.

The Cook Political Report predicted that voters in Arizona may lean Democrat in the general election this fall, which could help Hobbs grab her second term as governor in the purple state.

Hobbs won the 2022 gubernatorial election by flipping the seat blue in a margin of less than 1 percent. Two years later, President Donald Trump won the state in the 2024 presidential election.

Trump received 52.2 percent of the vote while former Vice President Kamala Harris got 46.7 percent.

Jeff Burton, Republican strategist and co-founding partner at Maven Advocacy, told The Epoch Times on July 17 that voters may tilt back to the left this fall.

“If the environment in November is similar to what it is now, it should be Hobbs’s race to lose,” Burton said.

The strategist suggested that some voter priorities may have shifted since Trump won the swing state in 2024.

“November, to some Republicans’ dismay, will be a nationalized election, and Republicans will be judged by whether voters feel better off: gas, groceries, rent and inflation will be most important,” Burton added.

The average cost for a regular gallon of gas in Arizona is up by more than $1 since last July, according to the American Automobile Association.

“Immigration and crime won’t be as salient as they were in 2024 when Trump carried Arizona,” Burton said.

The Epoch Times spoke to voters in Democrat-leaning Pima County and Republican-leaning Maricopa County on July 21 to get insights into their main concerns during the primary and general election.

In Maricopa County, where Phoenix is located, voters braved the sweltering 100 degree heat to cast their ballots.

Locals told The Epoch Times they were concerned about high rent, gas prices, and the rising cost of healthcare and education.

“Affordability is the name of the game,” Ben, a Democratic voter, said on July 21 outside of a polling center in Phoenix.

A poll worker, who did not want to be named, told The Epoch Times that voter turnout on July 21 was “good” and similar to previous elections.

The poll worker confirmed that mail-in ballots delivered to polling locations on Tuesday would be counted on the same day as opposed to later.

The new system is expected to speed up the counting process.

“The early voter ballots can now be tabulated on the same day as the vote-in-person process,” the worker added. “Normally, the early voter ballots that were submitted on election day were verified after election day.”

Meanwhile in Pima County, one of the main topics that energized voters to head to the polls on Tuesday in the town of Marana was how local leaders recently advanced two rezoning applications that could pave the way for approval of a data center.

Voters in the area had mixed reactions to the proposals, with locals on both sides telling The Epoch Times that the potential projects prompted them to vote for—or against—the town council members that sided with them on the potential developments.

One Republican voter suggested he went to the polls to show support for the town council, while another suggested it was time for new leadership.

“There’s a lot of talk about water usage and electricity concerns,” Olivia, a Republican, said. “It’s going to make our electricity bills go up and we don’t want that.”

The data center will use an air-cooled system, not a water-cooled system, and won’t cause water rates to increase, according to information about the proposed development on the town council’s website.

Council members added that they won’t regulate electricity of the data center because those agreements are controlled by the Arizona Corporation Commission.

“Utility agreements are structured to prevent the cost of serving the data center from being passed on to other customers,” the town council’s website added.

Looking ahead to the general election, Hobbs hopes to continue the efforts she made as governor in recent years.

In a campaign video on her website, she told voters that it was her idea to send the National Guard to the border in late 2023 to assist law enforcement with combating drugs from cartels, providing analytical support, and helping to stop human trafficking.

She also signed a budget that included a $1.4 billion tax cut for working families and made efforts to cut down the price of childcare.

Meanwhile, the Trump-endorsed Biggs polled strongly in the Republican primary and says he hopes to keep the momentum going into the fall.

One of his proposals is a plan to eliminate the state income tax.

The congressman said during the Republican gubernatorial debate on June 17 that eliminating the 2.5 percent income tax would entice businesses to move to the state rather than go to states with no income tax, such as Texas, Tennessee, and Florida.

Biggs, who questioned the results of the 2020 presidential election that sent former Vice President Joe Biden to the White House over Trump, is pushing for reforms to detect fraud and suggested that Arizona follow Florida’s lead on policies for a safe and secure election.
news.author: Jacki Thrapp

news.title: Error led to 400 noncitizens voting in New Jersey, governor says
news.text:
Roughly 400 noncitizens voted in New Jersey after about 6,600 residents were improperly registered to vote between June 2023 and June 2024 because of a software error at the Motor Vehicle Commission, Gov. Mikie Sherrill announced today.

All of the people affected had disclosed that they were not citizens when registering for either a driver’s license or ID, but were nonetheless placed on the voter rolls in error.

Those people will be removed from the voter rolls. The governor said that they may now face federal prosecution or unexpected hurdles — “through no fault of their own” — as they work toward becoming citizens.

“We are working very hard to protect them,” Sherrill, a Democrat who took office in January, said at a news conference.

The New York Times could not independently verify the figures disclosed Tuesday.

The software vendor Sherrill said was responsible for the error, Idemia, was being phased out of use in New Jersey, the governor said.

The state had “no evidence, at this time, that any elections were swayed,” she noted, including the 2024 presidential contest.

Officials with Idemia could not be immediately reached for comment.

Officials within the Sherrill administration became aware of the “serious software error” last week, the governor said, and began an immediate investigation into what had happened.

The error was discovered and corrected in 2024, while Phil Murphy was governor, but never publicly disclosed, Sherrill said.

A spokesperson for Murphy, a Democrat who served for two terms, did not reply to emails, calls or a text message.

Sherrill was unstinting in her criticism of the way the Murphy administration handled the problem, citing “reckless failures.”

“It’s unacceptable that the vendor released software with such a glaring error. It’s unacceptable that the MVC took a year to get this issue fixed. And it’s unacceptable that no one in the previous administration brought this to light, demanded accountability or took action when it happened years ago,” Sherrill said.

The finding that roughly 400 noncitizens had cast ballots in New Jersey is one of the larger confirmed instances of noncitizens voting in U.S. elections. But it remains an exceptionally small percentage of the overall vote total.

If those 400 noncitizens had all voted in the state’s local elections in 2023, that would reflect just 0.02% of the electorate. If they voted in the 2024 presidential election, those 400 noncitizens would make up about 0.009% of the state’s overall vote totals.

Kamala Harris, the former vice president who ran against President Donald Trump in 2024, won New Jersey by more than 252,000 votes.

The findings may nonetheless add fuel to claims by Trump and his allies that noncitizens are illegally on voter rolls nationwide and are casting ballots in elections in wide numbers, especially as the president makes a final pitch to Congress to pass the SAVE America Act. The act would limit mail voting and add a documentary proof of citizenship requirement to voting.

“Democrats and their media allies have repeatedly said it is impossible for noncitizens to register to vote, let alone cast a ballot,” a White House spokesperson, Abigail Jackson, said today in an email.

“American voters deserve to have confidence that our elections are safe and secure,” she added.

Joe Gruters, the chair of the Republican National Committee, said that the disclosure confirmed information the party had also learned through a public records request. Gruters, a state senator in Florida, said that it was further evidence of a need for Congress to pass the SAVE America Act.

“Democrats keep claiming this never happens,” Gruters said in a statement, “yet every new discovery tells the same old story. Enough excuses.”

Sherrill contrasted her decision to publicly disclose the error with what she called Trump’s efforts to “weaponize elections for political gain.”

“For the past 10 years, he’s cried wolf and peddled bizarre conspiracy theories about fraud with no evidence,” Sherrill said.

“Trump could care less about the actual integrity of the process,” she added. “His only concern is himself.”

This article originally appeared in The New York Times.

news.summary: Roughly 400 noncitizens voted in New Jersey after about 6,600 residents were improperly registered to vote between June 2023 and June 2024 because of a software error at the Motor Vehicle Commission, Gov. Mikie Sherrill announced today.
news.author: New York Times

news.title: President Trump to hold rally in Michigan next week
news.text:
WASHINGTON (AP) — President Donald Trump is set to rally in Michigan next week as early in-person voting begins in the battleground state’s primary, where voters will choose nominees for open Senate and governor’s races.

The rally is set to take place in Oakland County on Monday, according to a person familiar with the plans who was not authorized to discuss the event publicly.

Early in-person voting begins Saturday ahead of the Aug. 4 primary, when voters will also select nominees for U.S. House and other statewide offices. Republican Mike Rogers is unopposed in the Senate primary, while the GOP gubernatorial contest has intensified between Trump-backed U.S. Rep. John James and businessman Perry Johnson.

The visit will allow the president to take his messaging on the road as Republicans grow concerned about how rising gas prices and an unpopular war in Iran might affect their midterm chances. Republicans are defending slim majorities in the House and Senate.

Trump carried Michigan in 2016 and 2024, and both parties view the battleground state as central to the 2026 midterm elections.

The White House did not respond to a request for comment.

Democrats are facing the most competitive race in the Aug. 4 primary, with U.S. Rep. Haley Stevens and progressive candidate Abdul El-Sayed battling for the party’s Senate nomination. The winner will face Rogers, who lost narrowly in 2024 to now-Sen. Elissa Slotkin. 

Republicans have faced a bruising contest for the gubernatorial nomination, however, with Democratic Gov. Gretchen Whitmer term-limited. James has been endorsed by Trump, but Johnson has spent millions attacking James on television and digital advertising.

Johnson’s campaign has already advertised a “Trump Tailgate” on Monday. 

Oakland County, where Trump is set to appear, was long considered one of Michigan’s premier political bellwether counties. Democrats have steadily expanded their margins there even as Trump carried the state in 2016 and again in 2024.

Republicans saw 2026 as a chance to finally gain back power in a state that has been dominated by Democrats — outside of Trump’s wins — over the past decade. But surging gas prices, an unpopular war in Iran and tariffs that have hit the state’s auto industry hard have all contributed to concerns about a worsening political environment for Michigan Republicans.

In May, a Democrat won a special election by almost 20 points in a state Senate district that Kamala Harris won by less than 1 point in 2024. The previous officeholder, a Democrat, carried the seat by 6 percentage points in 2022.
news.author: Joey Cappelletti

news.title: Which direction is Trump’s job approval rating headed?
news.text:
ANALYSIS — The good news for Republicans is that President Donald Trump’s job approval rating isn’t getting any worse. The bad news is that many of the issues that drove his rating down in the first place are making their way back into the news, with the midterm elections creeping closer with each passing day.

After beginning his second term with a 52 percent job approval rating, according to Nate Silver’s national average, Trump embarked on a steady downward slide, slipping to a low of 38 percent near the end of May. Since that low point, the president has seen modest improvement to 40 percent, as of Monday. 

And GOP strategists have been encouraged by some improvement for their party in the generic congressional ballot, which asks respondents whether they plan to vote for the Republican or Democratic candidate for Congress. They specifically point to the latest Washington Post-Ipsos poll, which had Democrats with a 3-point advantage, even though Silver’s average has shown Democrats with a consistent 6-point edge for the past five months. 

Republicans, who are defending narrow majorities on Capitol Hill, are looking for any bits of hope, given the traditional midterm headwinds they face and Trump being in worse shape now compared with his first term. At his lowest point, in mid-2017, Trump was upside down by 20 points on his job approval rating, but he bounced up to being 13 points underwater ahead of his first midterm, when Republicans lost a net of 40 House seats (and netted two Senate seats). 

This time around, Trump is underwater by 22 points, according to G. Elliott Morris' polling average, and is almost six months behind from when he started his 2018 rebound. And even though the president appears to have halted his slide, another downturn looks more likely than a rebound. 

Trump is at risk of squandering the goodwill and enthusiasm from hosting the World Cup and the “America 250” celebration with a series of policy decisions that contributed to his initial political decline.

Despite multiple claims of ceasefires and agreements, the conflict with Iran is back on. Three more U.S. servicemembers have died over the past week, two as a result of a missile attack on an air base in Jordan and one during an Iranian drone detonation at a northern Iraq air base. That has brought the total number of service members killed since the war began in February to 17 as of Tuesday afternoon. And uncertainty over the Strait of Hormuz has pushed the national average price of gas back up to $4 per gallon, according to AAA as of Tuesday.

Two more people were fatally shot by Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents in Texas and Maine, prompting the agency to largely suspend vehicle stops. But Trump promptly reversed that policy reversal. “[We] must be strong, tough, and smart, and we CANNOT give up one of I.C.E.’s most important and effective Crime Fighting tools, THE TRAFFIC STOP!” Trump posted on Truth Social. 

And, unfortunately, more violence related to immigration enforcement appears likely, based on the agency’s track record.

Last week, the president chose to utilize a prime-time address to relitigate his 2020 election loss. Trump’s focus on vote-counting in Georgia has frustrated some Republicans, right from the immediate aftermath of the 2020 election when his fixation contributed to the GOP losing both of Georgia’s Senate seats in January 2021 runoffs. And the president on Monday signed proclamations imposing 50 percent tariffs on certain goods from Canada, a longtime ally that also incurred Trump’s wrath over wildfire smoke drifting from across the northern border.

In total, it almost reads like the greatest hits of Trump’s most unpopular political positions. 

The uncertainty around Trump’s standing comes at a critical moment. Not only are the midterm elections a little more than three months away, but both parties are polling in earnest in dozens of races, calibrating their fall strategies. So even if Republicans are feeling cautiously optimistic about the latest data, that could turn out to be a mirage if the president’s job approval rating takes another turn for the worse. And seats that look solid for the GOP today may not be in a month or two from now. 

But Republicans aren’t waiting on Trump to get dramatically more popular. Rather than the election being a referendum on the president, they’re intent on it framing November as a choice between the two parties. More specifically, Speaker Mike Johnson described it as a “contrast between common sense and crazy” at a recent campaign event for Omaha City Council Member Brinker Harding, who’s trying to succeed Republican Don Bacon in Nebraska’s battleground 2nd District. 

It’s a good strategy considering that the national Democratic Party isn’t popular either. Overall, Republicans believe Democrats are making the wrong choices in key primaries around the country. 

Some of the minority party’s highest-profile primary winners have been affiliated with the Democratic Socialists of America. Even though the seats they won aren’t the most competitive, Republicans plan to portray them as the de facto leaders of the Democratic Party. 

Back in 2018, Republicans held onto Nebraska’s 2nd District, 51 percent to 49 percent, in the face of a Democratic wave after the more progressive candidate won the Democratic primary. But Bacon, a retired Air Force brigadier general, was an established incumbent that cycle, while Harding is trying to win an open seat. The district has also become more Democratic over the past decade. Trump would have finished ahead of Hillary Clinton by 2 points in the 2016 election, under the district’s current lines, while Kamala Harris finished ahead of Trump by 5 points in 2024. 

In the end, the outcome of the midterm elections likely comes back to the president’s standing and how voters feel about the direction of the country. And while it looked just a few weeks ago like Trump and the Republicans were poised for a modest bump, the president who can’t seem to get out of his own way could be headed for another downward turn once again.
news.author: Nathan L. Gonzales

news.title: Louisiana’s Cleo Fields forgoes reelection after redistricting eliminated his seat
news.text:
Louisiana Democrat Cleo Fields said Tuesday that he would not seek reelection this fall after his majority-Black district was redrawn to favor Republicans earlier this year. 

Instead, Fields said he would seek a return to the state Senate, running for his old seat that’s newly open following the recent death of his successor.

“After a great deal of prayer and reflection, I have decided not to seek re-election to Congress, in the 6th District or any other district in Louisiana,” he said in a statement. “I feel my calling to serve the people is better put to use back home than here in D.C., and I intend to answer that call.”

Fields returned to Congress last year nearly three decades after he served two terms in the mid-1990s before retiring after court-ordered redistricting. After leaving Congress, he served two separate stints in the state Senate before a new congressional map was approved in 2024, clearing the way for his return to the House from a redrawn district that stretched from Baton Rouge to Shreveport. 

But in April, the Supreme Court struck down that map, which had given Democrats two majority-Black House seats, finding that it was an unconstitutional racial gerrymander. The state Legislature then adopted new congressional lines, while also delaying the primaries for House races until November. All candidates, regardless of party, will compete on the same ballot, with the two top finishers advancing to Dec. 12 runoffs if no one wins an outright majority. 

Under the new map, the 6th District currently held by Fields, which Kamala Harris carried by 15 points, is essentially dismantled. Trump would have won the new 6th District — a majority-white seat in the Baton Rouge area — by 32 points, according to calculations by Inside Elections with Nathan L. Gonzales. 

Fields’ announcement comes a day after the National Republican Congressional Committee added Louisiana’s 6th District to its list of targeted House seats this fall.

“This fall, Louisianans in the Sixth District will elect a strong, commonsense conservative who will give them the representation they deserve,” NRCC spokesman Reilly Richardson said in a statement.
news.author: Mary Ellen McIntire

news.title: ‘Unacceptable’: Governor admits thousands of noncitizens registered to vote
news.text:
The governor of New Jersey has admitted that thousands and thousands of noncitizens were registered to vote in her state, and many of them actually voted, illegally, in a U.S. election, but it’s all the fault of a software error.

A report at Politico said Gov. Mikie Sherrill said more than 6,000 people who are not U.S. citizens were added to her state’s voter rolls.

She claimed it was minor because “fewer than 400” actually voted.

Last week I learned that a serious software error in New Jersey’s Motor Vehicle System led to the registration of roughly 6,600 people who indicated that they were not U.S. citizens between June 2023 and June 2024, almost three years prior to my taking office. These individuals…
— Governor Mikie Sherrill (@GovSherrillNJ) July 21, 2026

BREAKING: New Jersey Gov. Mikie Sherrill says a software glitch in the state’s motor vehicle system mistakenly registered about 6,600 non-U.S. citizens to vote when they applied for driver’s licenses or state ID cards.
According to Sherrill, the applicants correctly indicated… pic.twitter.com/ZODKaGUdJ0
— Fox News (@FoxNews) July 21, 2026

New Jersey mistakenly registered 6,600 apparent noncitizens to vote over the course of a year, including hundreds who went on to improperly cast ballots, according to Gov. Mikie Sherrill (D). https://t.co/RcCq8fqN9F
— The Washington Post (@washingtonpost) July 21, 2026

A Gateway Pundit comment pointed out Sherrill is an “emerging” star for the Democrats and her confirmation of the blunder “vindicates those who have pushed for the SAVE America Act and investigating voter fraud that has occurred in previous eletions.”

Sherrill’s online admission confirmed she recently learned of the disaster caused by an “error” in the software used by the state’s motor vehicles system.

“This ‘coincidentally’ occurred right in the midst of the 2024 election campaign. Sherrill, of course, tried to assure that the impact was ‘minor,'” the report said. “Last week I learned that a serious software error in New Jersey’s Motor Vehicle System led to the registration of roughly 6,600 people who indicated that they were not U.S. citizens between June 2023 and June 2024, almost three years prior to my taking office.”

The confirmation comes amid an effort by the administration of President Donald Trump to crack down on illegal voting.

Sherrill, a Democrat, said the mistake happened during the application process for driver’s licenses and ID cards.

Thousands confirmed they were not citizens but were given voting rights in the state anyway.

Sherrill claimed there’s no immediate proof that any of the roughly 400 non-citizens who cast ballots impacted the outcome of any individual election, the report said.

“We have no evidence at this time that any elections were swayed,” she said.

She said, “It’s unacceptable that the vendor released software with such a glaring error, it’s unacceptable that the MVC took a year to get this issue fixed and it’s unacceptable that no one in the previous administration brought this to light, demanded accountability, or took action when it happened years ago.”

The reaction from the White House wasn’t a surprise. Spokeswoman Abigail Jackson said, “Democrats and their media allies have repeatedly said it is impossible for noncitizens to register to vote, let alone cast a ballot. Time and again, they have been proven wrong. As President Trump has said, there is nothing more important than the integrity of our elections. And this latest incident underscores the absolute necessity of the SAVE America Act.”

Republicans in the state long have warned of such events, but have been blocked recently by leftist judge.

Sherrill suggested she would give a pass to those who voted illegally, and veered off-topic long enough to overlook President Trump’s call for election integrity and rant about how she thinks he was working to “undermine” the vote.

Said the Pundit, “If the Democratic governor of New Jersey is admitting that foreign nationals illegally voted in New Jersey elections, then one can bet this is just the tip of the iceberg. This also raises the question of whether voter fraud played a significant role in Kamala Harris winning the Garden State in 2024, which was unexpectedly close.”
news.summary: 'One can bet this is just the tip of the iceberg'
news.author: Bob Unruh

news.title: Laura Loomer Apologizes For Downplaying Ukraine War: 'I Feel Like Such An A**hole'
news.text:
Laura Loomer just did something rare for a MAGA lover: admit she was wrong.

The right-wing personality, conspiracy theorist and noted Donald Trump ally is in Ukraine to witness the war with Russia after she last month said on her podcast that she might have been a victim of Russian propaganda. She previously argued against U.S. funding for Ukraine after the Russian invasion and called Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy a “Jihadi apologist.”

She posted Tuesday on X that experiencing an air raid in the war-torn country shifted her opinions on the conflict.

Loomer said after hearing “sirens blaring” and realizing “this is everyday for every Ukrainian,” she now feels “like such an asshole for minimizing the struggle of Ukrainians for the last 5 years.”

“I often said I don’t care. Looking back, that wasn’t very nice of me to say.There are people in Ukraine who have to run to a bomb shelter everyday and some of us had the audacity to say Russia wasn’t wrong because we believed the lie that Ukraine is a country full of Nazi sympathizers.We have been so propagandized by Russia and we don’t even realize it.”

Loomer then used her moment of clarity to shift things back to her normally bonkers content, claiming “air sirens could very well be the future of America if we don’t start demanding our lawmakers crack down on communism and multipolarity,” and warned, “we won’t have Trump forever and the axis of evil is moving fast.”

HuffPost reached out to the White House for comment on the post, but no one immediately responded.

Zelenskyy thanked Loomer in a response post, saying, “It is really important” she is in his country and “sees things with her own eyes as they are.”

Loomer isn’t known for fact-based commentary. She has spread conspiracy theories about Sept. 11 being an inside job, that former Vice President Kamala Harris is not Black and that Ukrainian militants were in the crowds during the Jan. 6, 2021, riot.
news.author: David Moye
